author: rodrigallardo
title: [Feat]: Adding Artifacts to TaskSendParams
raw_text:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

_No response_

### Describe the solution you'd like

Hi, everyone!

Our team @ LATAM Airlines has been working on developing agents and agent-to-agent communication for a while, and one of the needs we identified was sharing artifacts between agents. 

An agent could leverage an artifact generated by another agent for its operation. Say, for example, you've got an image generation agent for a specific use case (e.g. marketing) called Marketing Agent, and another agent in charge of handling a social network account like a company's LinkedIn Profile, called LinkedIn Agent. A reasonable use case would be to use both agents through an orchestrator and ask to generate LinkedIn posts with images based on natural language prompts. The LinkedIn agent would require an image as input, which the orchestrator would provide after asking the Marketing Agent to generate it. We've identified many other use cases that require agents to receive artifacts as inputs, preprocess them, and use them as arguments for different tools or as inputs to LLMs.

An Artifact on the TaskSendParams is not particularly needed to accomplish this: one could copy the parts of the Artifact on a Message object and send that, and the A2A Server would receive all it needs. However, it would be beneficial if the Artifact object remained immutable when forwarded to another Agent for traceability, observability, and monitoring purposes. That way, one could trace which agents used the same Artifact as input, or the whole lifespan of an Artifact as shared by the orchestrator or other agents.

It would also be useful for implementing something like an Artifact Store. If the same Artifact object representing an agent's result was shared between multiple agents, these objects could all point to the same object on a remote Artifact Store, which would act as a central repository for all artifacts generated by agents on a multi-agent system.

I would like to know your thoughts on this!

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_

author: everettVT
title: Python DEMO Step 8: Incomplete/Confusing code update to  _stream_3_messages() method
raw_text:
In the Python Demo there are a few issues: 

First, the dot-env dependency needs to be added in step 5, such that instead of 

```python
uv add click
```
We need to add python-dotenv

``` python
uv add click python-dotenv
```
---

In Step 8, the second task_manager.py alteration asks users to go from

```python 
import asyncio
# ...
class MyAgentTaskManager(InMemoryTaskManager):
  # ...
  async def _stream_3_messages(self, request: SendTaskStreamingRequest):
    task_id = request.params.id
    received_text = request.params.message.parts[0].text

    text_messages = ["one", "two", "three"]
    for text in text_messages:
      parts = [
        {
          "type": "text",
          "text": f"{received_text}: {text}",
        }
      ]
      message = Message(role="agent", parts=parts)
      is_last = text == text_messages[-1]
      task_state = TaskState.COMPLETED if is_last else TaskState.WORKING
      task_status = TaskStatus(
        state=task_state,
        message=message
      )
      task_update_event = TaskStatusUpdateEvent(
        id=request.params.id,
        status=task_status,
        final=is_last,
      )
      await self.enqueue_events_for_sse(
        request.params.id,
        task_update_event
      )

  async def on_send_task_subscribe(
    self,
    request: SendTaskStreamingRequest
  ) -> AsyncIterable[SendTaskStreamingResponse] | JSONRPCResponse:
    # Upsert a task stored by InMemoryTaskManager
    await self.upsert_task(request.params)

    task_id = request.params.id
    # Create a queue of work to be done for this task
    sse_event_queue = await self.setup_sse_consumer(task_id=task_id)

    # Start the asynchronous work for this task
    asyncio.create_task(self._stream_3_messages(request))

    # Tell the client to expect future streaming responses
    return self.dequeue_events_for_sse(
      request_id=request.id,
      task_id=task_id,
      sse_event_queue=sse_event_queue,
    )
```

to 

```python
# ...

class MyAgentTaskManager(InMemoryTaskManager):
  # ...
  async def _stream_3_messages(self, request: SendTaskStreamingRequest):
    # ...
    async for message in messages:
      # ...
      # is_last = message == messages[-1] # Delete this line
      task_state = TaskState.WORKING
      # ...
      task_update_event = TaskStatusUpdateEvent(
        id=request.params.id,
        status=task_status,
        final=False,
      )
      # ...

    ask_message = Message(
      role="agent",
      parts=[
        {
          "type": "text",
          "text": "Would you like more messages? (Y/N)"
        }
      ]
    )
    task_update_event = TaskStatusUpdateEvent(
      id=request.params.id,
      status=TaskStatus(
        state=TaskState.INPUT_REQUIRED,
        message=ask_message
      ),
      final=True,
    )
    await self.enqueue_events_for_sse(
      request.params.id,
      task_update_event
    )
  # ...
  async def on_send_task_subscribe(
    self,
    request: SendTaskStreamingRequest
  ) -> AsyncIterable[SendTaskStreamingResponse] | JSONRPCResponse:
    task_id = request.params.id
    is_new_task = task_id in self.tasks
    # Upsert a task stored by InMemoryTaskManager
    await self.upsert_task(request.params)

    received_text = request.params.message.parts[0].text
    sse_event_queue = await self.setup_sse_consumer(task_id=task_id)
    if not is_new_task and received_text == "N":
      task_update_event = TaskStatusUpdateEvent(
        id=request.params.id,
        status=TaskStatus(
          state=TaskState.COMPLETED,
          message=Message(
            role="agent",
            parts=[
              {
                "type": "text",
                "text": "All done!"
              }
            ]
          )
        ),
        final=True,
      )
      await self.enqueue_events_for_sse(
        request.params.id,
        task_update_event,
      )
    else:
      asyncio.create_task(self._stream_3_messages(request))

    return self.dequeue_events_for_sse(
      request_id=request.id,
      task_id=task_id,
      sse_event_queue=sse_event_queue,
    )

```

### Without explanations for why we are adding an `async` to the `for` loop and altering the looping variables from `text_messages` to `messages`. 


```python 
text_messages = ["one", "two", "three"]
for text in text_messages:
    parts = [
        {
            "type": "text",
            "text": f"{received_text}: {message}",
        }
    ]
    message = Message(role="agent", parts=parts)
    task_state = TaskState.WORKING
    task_status = TaskStatus(
        state=task_state,
        message=message
    )
...
```
to 
```python
async for message in messages:
        parts = [
            {
                "type": "text",
                "text": f"{received_text}: {message}",
            }
        ]
        message = Message(role="agent", parts=parts)
        task_state = TaskState.WORKING
        task_status = TaskStatus(
            state=task_state,
            message=message
        )
...
```

Are supposed to get rid of our explicit text_messages? Obviously not according to the expected outputs. Why do either of those changes? 

This then leads to a confusion on the indentation, and the general workflow of the entire method since the async for loop actually only extends to the end of the TaskStatusUpdateEvent such that the message for input required is outside the loop. 

```python
    task_update_event = TaskStatusUpdateEvent(
        id=request.params.id,
        status=task_status,
        final=False,
    )
# HERE
ask_message = Message(
    role="agent",
    parts=[
        {
            "type": "text",
            "text": "Would you like more messages? (Y/N)"
        }
    ]
)
```

author: darkhaniop
title: Incorrect field type in `TaskResubscriptionRequest` (types.py)
raw_text:
### Problem

In `common/types.py`, field `params` should be of type `TaskQueryParams`.

https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/b52c2ebb381f2769159af90e35ac975313f59fd4/samples/python/common/types.py#L222-L224

### Details

The difference between `TaskIdParams` and `TaskQueryParams` is only the optional `historyLength` field. However, the A2A spec (`a2a.json`) and the TypeScript sample (`schema.ts`) indicate that this field should be of type `TaskQueryParams`.

https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/b52c2ebb381f2769159af90e35ac975313f59fd4/samples/js/src/schema.ts#L717-L727

https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/b52c2ebb381f2769159af90e35ac975313f59fd4/specification/json/a2a.json#L1866-L1874

author: robert-at-pretension-io
title: I wrote a typesafe rust A2A server and client with over 160 passing unit/integration tests... did I miss any?
raw_text:
Here's a breakdown of what the server-side unit and integration tests verify, grouped by feature:

**Task Repository (In-Memory Implementation)**

*   `task_repository_test.rs`
    *   Tests saving a task and then retrieving it successfully by its ID.
    *   Tests deleting a saved task prevents subsequent retrieval.
    *   Tests saving and retrieving push notification configuration associated with a task ID.
    *   Tests that retrieving push notification config for a task without one returns nothing.
    *   Tests saving multiple state updates for a task and retrieving the ordered history.
    *   Tests retrieving state history for a task that never had any returns an empty list.

**Task Service Logic**

*   `task_service_test.rs`
    *   Verifies processing a valid task request saves the task and returns it (mock completes immediately).
    *   Verifies tasks sent with specific IDs, sessions, and metadata are persisted correctly.
    *   Verifies sending a follow-up to a task not in `InputRequired` state returns an error.
    *   Verifies retrieving an existing task by ID returns the correct data.
    *   Verifies retrieving a non-existent task by ID returns a `TaskNotFound` error.
    *   Verifies canceling a 'Working' task correctly transitions its state to 'Canceled'.
    *   Verifies attempting to cancel an already 'Completed' task returns a `TaskNotCancelable` error.
    *   Verifies sending a follow-up message to an 'InputRequired' task transitions it towards completion.
    *   Verifies retrieving the state history for a task returns the correct sequence of states.
    *   Verifies getting state history for a non-existent task returns a `TaskNotFound` error.

**Notification Service Logic**

*   `notification_service_test.rs`
    *   Verifies successfully setting a basic push notification URL for an existing task.
    *   Verifies successfully setting a push notification URL along with authentication details.
    *   Verifies attempting to set push notifications for a non-existent task returns a `TaskNotFound` error.
    *   Verifies retrieving push notification details returns the correct URL and authentication info.
    *   Verifies retrieving push notification details for a task where none were set returns an appropriate error.
    *   Verifies retrieving push notification details for a non-existent task returns a `TaskNotFound` error.

**Streaming Service Logic**

*   `streaming_service_test.rs`
    *   Verifies creating a streaming task returns a stream with correct SSE formatting and JSON-RPC structure.
    *   Verifies resubscribing to an active task provides a stream starting with the current state.
    *   Verifies resubscribing to a completed task provides a stream containing the final 'Completed' state.
    *   Verifies attempting to resubscribe to a non-existent task returns a `TaskNotFound` error.

**JSON-RPC Handler Logic & Edge Cases**

*   `jsonrpc_handler_test.rs`
    *   Verifies the handler returns correct JSON-RPC errors for Method Not Found, Invalid Parameters, and Parse Error.
    *   Tests handling of `tasks/send` with extremely large text input.
    *   Tests handling of `tasks/send` with invalid UTF-8 text input.
    *   Tests handling of `tasks/send` with deeply nested metadata structures.
    *   Tests handling of `tasks/send` where optional fields are explicitly set to `null`.
    *   Verifies sending a follow-up via `tasks/send` to a 'Working' task fails correctly.
    *   Verifies sending a follow-up via `tasks/send` to a 'Canceled' task fails correctly.
    *   Verifies sending a follow-up via `tasks/send` to a 'Failed' task fails correctly.
    *   Verifies sending a task via `tasks/send` with non-object metadata in a part fails correctly.
    *   Verifies sending a task via `tasks/send` with a part missing the `type` field fails correctly.
    *   Tests `tasks/send` ensuring the top-level JSON-RPC `id` is used in the response, even if different from `params.id`.
    *   Verifies `tasks/sendSubscribe` with an invalid message structure returns a JSON-RPC error (not SSE).
    *   Verifies `tasks/sendSubscribe` for a task requiring input returns a valid SSE stream.
    *   Tests `tasks/sendSubscribe` when the client incorrectly sends `Accept: application/json`, ensuring SSE is still returned.
    *   Verifies `tasks/sendSubscribe` with an empty message `parts` array returns a valid SSE stream.
    *   Tests `tasks/get` retrieving a task in the 'Submitted' state.
    *   Tests `tasks/get` retrieving a task in the 'InputRequired' state.
    *   Tests `tasks/get` retrieving a task in the 'Failed' state.
    *   Tests `tasks/get` ignores extra, unknown parameters in the request.
    *   Verifies `tasks/cancel` correctly cancels a task in the 'InputRequired' state.
    *   Verifies `tasks/cancel` on an already 'Canceled' task returns a `TaskNotCancelable` error.
    *   Verifies `tasks/cancel` on a non-existent task returns a `TaskNotFound` error.
    *   Tests `tasks/cancel` ignores extra, unknown parameters in the request.
    *   Verifies `tasks/pushNotification/set` with an invalid URL format returns an Invalid Parameters error.
    *   Verifies `tasks/pushNotification/set` correctly overwrites a previously existing configuration.
    *   Tests `tasks/pushNotification/set` with an empty array for authentication schemes.
    *   Tests `tasks/pushNotification/set` where authentication credentials are explicitly `null`.
    *   Verifies `tasks/pushNotification/set` can be called even for an already 'Completed' task.
    *   Verifies `tasks/pushNotification/get` for a task without config returns an appropriate error.
    *   Verifies `tasks/pushNotification/get` for a non-existent task returns a `TaskNotFound` error.
    *   Tests `tasks/pushNotification/get` ignores extra, unknown parameters in the request.
    *   Verifies the `tasks/send` endpoint successfully creates and returns a task.
    *   Verifies the `tasks/get` endpoint successfully retrieves an existing task.
    *   Verifies the `tasks/get` endpoint returns `TaskNotFound` for non-existent IDs.
    *   Verifies the `tasks/cancel` endpoint successfully cancels a task.
    *   Verifies the `tasks/sendSubscribe` endpoint initiates a task and returns an SSE stream.
    *   Verifies the `tasks/resubscribe` endpoint connects to a task and returns an SSE stream.
    *   Verifies the `tasks/pushNotification/set` endpoint saves configuration (via mock repo).
    *   Verifies the `/.well-known/agent.json` endpoint returns a valid agent card structure.
    *   Tests `tasks/send` with an invalid message structure (e.g., missing role) fails correctly.
    *   Tests `tasks/send` with an empty `parts` array is handled (mock allows it).
    *   Tests `tasks/send` with an invalid `part.type` is handled (mock allows it).
    *   Tests `tasks/send` with non-object `metadata` fails correctly.
    *   Tests `tasks/get` with a non-string ID fails correctly.
    *   Tests `tasks/cancel` with a non-string ID fails correctly.
    *   Tests `tasks/sendSubscribe` with an invalid message structure fails correctly.
    *   Tests `tasks/resubscribe` with a non-string ID fails correctly.
    *   Tests `tasks/pushNotification/set` with an invalid config structure fails correctly.
    *   Tests `tasks/pushNotification/get` with a non-string ID fails correctly.
    *   Tests calling the non-standard `tasks/stateHistory/get` with a non-string ID fails.
    *   Tests sending a GET request to the POST-only endpoint results in a Parse Error.
    *   Tests sending a request with incorrect `Content-Type` is still processed (but fails later).
    *   Tests the race condition between concurrently canceling and sending a follow-up to a task.
    *   Tests server behavior when the repository fails mid-task-processing (e.g., on second save).
    *   Tests the race condition of canceling a task immediately after initiating its stream.
    *   Tests setting/getting push notifications with highly complex nested JSON in `authentication.extra`.
    *   Tests how conflicting mock control metadata (e.g., `_mock_require_input`) is handled.
    *   Tests the race condition of canceling a task immediately after resubscribing to it.
    *   Tests `tasks/get` when requested `history_length` exceeds available history entries.
    *   Tests the race condition of concurrently sending two `tasks/send` requests with the same new ID.
    *   Tests sending requests with malformed (but valid JSON) JSON-RPC structures.
    *   Tests sending a follow-up message with an incorrect `role` (e.g., 'agent').

**Server Integration (End-to-End)**

*   `server_integration_test.rs`
    *   Tests the basic workflow: get agent card -> send task -> get task.
    *   Verifies a simple send -> get task sequence results in a 'Completed' state.
    *   Verifies send -> cancel -> get results in a 'Canceled' state.
    *   Verifies getting a completed task shows 'Completed' state.
    *   Verifies getting a canceled task shows 'Canceled' state.
    *   Tests the full input-required flow: send -> get (InputRequired) -> follow-up -> get (Completed).
    *   Verifies sending a follow-up to a 'Completed' task fails correctly.
    *   Verifies sending a follow-up to a 'Canceled' task fails correctly.
    *   Verifies `tasks/get` for a non-existent ID returns `TaskNotFound`.
    *   Verifies `tasks/cancel` for a non-existent ID returns `TaskNotFound`.
    *   Verifies `tasks/cancel` for a 'Completed' task returns `TaskNotCancelable`.
    *   Verifies `tasks/cancel` for an already 'Canceled' task returns `TaskNotCancelable`.
    *   Tests sending a structurally invalid `tasks/send` request returns `InvalidParameters`.
    *   Verifies basic `tasks/sendSubscribe` produces updates and completes.
    *   Tests canceling a task during `tasks/sendSubscribe` results in a 'Canceled' final stream update.
    *   Tests the input-required flow initiated via `tasks/sendSubscribe` (stream consumption simplified).
    *   Tests resubscribing to a working task (stream consumption simplified).
    *   Verifies resubscribing to a 'Completed' task provides the final 'Completed' update.
    *   Verifies resubscribing to a 'Canceled' task provides the final 'Canceled' update.
    *   (Ignored) Intended to test resubscribing to a non-existent task returns an error.
    *   Verifies metadata sent with a task appears in stream updates.
    *   Tests setting and retrieving push notification configuration and details.
    *   Verifies setting push notifications for a non-existent task fails correctly.
    *   Verifies getting push notifications for a non-existent task fails correctly.
    *   Verifies getting push notifications when none are set fails correctly.
    *   Verifies setting push notifications multiple times overwrites the previous setting.
    *   (Simplified) Intended to test setting invalid push notification configs.
    *   Tests the get/cancel concurrent race condition.
    *   Tests the cancel/resubscribe concurrent race condition.
    *   Tests the follow-up/cancel concurrent race condition.
    *   Tests sending a task with a file attachment completes successfully.
    *   Tests sending a task with structured data completes successfully.
    *   Tests retrieving state history and verifies basic transition order.


If you can think of any tests I need to add, please let me know. 

My next "trick" will be making an ai controller that is able to connect A2A requests to MCP's -- essentially allowing A2A to be an easy-to-setup wrapper for mcp servers.

I think this will drive adoption -- especially when payment schemes can be added so companies have an incentive to implement A2A services.

author: logan272
title: Proposal: Create a Separate GitHub Organization for A2A
raw_text:
I propose establishing a dedicated GitHub organization for hosting the A2A specification and the official A2A SDK repositories.

Currently, both the Python and JavaScript A2A SDKs reside in the same repository, and neither is published as packages. This setup complicates community contributions and usability.

Creating a separate GitHub organization for A2A would allow for better organization and management of the SDKs, making it easier for the community to use and contribute to them.

author: laurentsimon
title: Harden notification service
raw_text:
In the samples (and the specs afaict), the client of the agent is not authenticated; only the agent is. See https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/main/samples/python/hosts/cli/push_notification_listener.py#L51-L58, which echos the validation token.
The specs say "This seems simple but it helps avoid tricking remote agent into DDOS-ing a URL by a malicious client." without mentioning the need to authenticate the URL owner.

In practice, an attacker may submit the URL of a known victim's push notification receiver to DoS it or inject logs. The notification receiver will always reply with the validation token and thus the agent will send it further notifications.


Another potential attack vector is the use of error messages returned by agent to client. An attacker may submit arbitrary URLs to the agent in an attempt to identify internal domains / IPs that exist within the local network the agent runs on (internal enterprise network). Example: for a given push notification URL, the agent may return "domain not available" or "failed token verification" which would allow to distinguish and start mapping an internal network. The current samples are not vulnerable to this afaict since the exceptions are caught and only logged, but another implementation may make this mistake. It may be useful to call this out in implementation guidelines

author: robert-at-pretension-io
title: Enabling Cross-Provider Monetization in A2A
raw_text:
For the A2A ecosystem to thrive, agents need ways to charge for services. However, requiring users/agents to manage separate payment accounts for *every* provider creates significant friction, hindering the vision of a seamless agent web.

How can the protocol facilitate monetization *without* becoming overly complex, especially early on?

**Conceptual Ideas:**

1.  **Auth Token = Billing Key:** Use existing auth; providers handle billing externally. (Simple, but doesn't solve the user's multi-provider hassle).
2.  **Metadata Payment Info:** Agents include standard payment pointers/invoices in `Task.metadata` upon completion. (Low protocol impact, relies on client agents to handle payment, post-pay risk).
3.  **Metadata Credits:** Pass verifiable credit tokens (from trusted issuers) in `metadata`. (Low-medium impact, starts solving user hassle, needs issuer ecosystem).
4.  **Formal Pricing/Commitment:** Add `pricing` to `AgentSkill`, pass payment proof (`commitmentToken`) in requests. (Medium impact, schema change, better provider assurance).
5.  **Formal Metering/Escrow:** More complex protocol extensions for detailed billing or secure escrow flows. (High impact, significant complexity).

**Key Trade-off:** Balancing ease of adoption (simpler protocol) vs. solving the user's cross-provider payment problem more directly.

**Proposal for Initial Steps:**

*   Acknowledge **option 1** as the baseline.
*   Promote **option 2 (Metadata Payment Info)** as the least disruptive *first step*. It allows experimentation and client-side payment abstraction using existing standards, without immediate, complex protocol changes.
*   View **option 4 (Formal Pricing)** and others as potential *future* enhancements once the protocol gains traction.

This prioritizes adoption while offering a flexible path forward via metadata.

**Questions for Discussion:**

*   Is this the right initial balance?
*   Are metadata-based approaches sufficient for now?
*   What are the priorities for monetization support?

Looking forward to community input! ( if you're a reddit user, check out the fledgling /r/AgentToAgent subreddit. )

author: aickin
title: The semantics of Artifact updates are underspecified in the technical documentation
raw_text:
I read through the documentation about artifact updates in the [Streaming Support](https://google.github.io/A2A/#/documentation?id=streaming-support) section, and it feels like this section needs to be clearer about how updates happen. Here are some questions I have that I don't think are currently answered:

1. First off, are names for Artifacts unique in the scope of a Task? Are indexes unique? Is it allowed in the spec to have two Artifacts in a Task with the same name but different indexes? Conversely, is it allowed in the spec to have two Artifacts in a Task with different names but the same index?
2. Assuming that index is unique in the scope of a Task, what do you do when a TaskArtifactUpdateEvent sends a new `name` for the Artifact? Does it overwrite the previous name? Is it an error? Or is it ignored? Similarly, is a new `description` for the Artifact an overwrite/error/ignored?
3. When you send metadata in a TaskArtifactUpdateEvent, does it replace the existing metadata for the Artifact, or is it merged into the existing metadata for the Artifact on a field-by-field basis, or is it an error, or is it ignored?
4. How do `append` and `lastChunk` work in practice? A few examples that I'm unsure about:
    1. Let's say you send one update with `append: false` and `lastChunk: false`, and then send a second update again with `append: false` and `lastChunk: false`. Intuitively, I would think that this wipes out and overwrites the first update. Is that correct, or is this an error?
    2. Now, let's say you send several updates in a row with `append: true` and `lastChunk: false`. Then you send an update with `append: true` and `lastChunk: true`. Finally you send an update with `append: true` and `lastChunk:false`. Is that an error?
    3. Similar to the last one,  let's say you send several updates in a row with `append: true` and `lastChunk: false`. Then you send an update with `append: true` and `lastChunk: true`. Finally you send an update with `append: false` and `lastChunk:false`. Is that an error or does it wipe out all the previous updates?

I know that some of these may seem like nitpicks, but I tend to think a spec should be really clear about error cases and state transitions. I think in it's current state, a reasonable engineer could assume several different ways this artifact update mechanism works.

author: logan272
title: Transport Protocol Abstraction
raw_text:
Currently, A2A uses SSE as its transport protocol. It would be beneficial to create a A2A transport protocol abstraction, allowing for the easy implementation and use of different transport channels.

The MCP protocol implements a transport protocol abstraction, with concrete transport channels such as Stdio, SSE, and InMemory built with this abstraction. 


https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/transports

author: logan272
title: How can multiple agents collaboratively handle a single A2A task?
raw_text:
After reading the spec and code samples, I still don't have a clear ideal how multiple agents can work together on a single task. According to the A2A specifications, it seems that an A2A task is intended to be handled by a single agent from start to finish.


Related issue: #215

author: rajib76
title: Interaction between agents..
raw_text:
In the documentation, it suggests that agents should collaborate with other agents using a2a protocol. But should we be thinking on this interaction as below:

There are two types of agent to agent interaction

1. Intra agency interaction: In this interaction, all the agents belong to the same orchestration, each is doing one part of a complex workflow. In this pattern, it makes sense to use a2a protocol to communicate among the agents.
2. Inter agency interaction : In this pattern, one or more agents within a orchestration may need some additional context from a 3rd party agent(many be on salesforce) or from another agency. In this situation, I think it makes more sense to use the agent as a tool and leverage MCP to get the context from the 3rd party agents. 

I would like to hear other's opinion on this. if this is acceptable, may be we can change the documentation accordingly.

author: whysage
title: Suggestion: Add scheduled task support
raw_text:
### Background
Now A2A only supports one-off task submission (via tasks/send) and real-time updates. Many integrations need the ability to schedule recurring work—e.g. “every night at 2 AM, run this task”—without external cron glue. We should extend the A2A protocol with first-class “scheduled tasks”:

- Clients can create, list, update, and delete schedules
- Schedules drive repeated invocation of the existing task execution engine
- Scheduling syntax should be familiar (cron-style expressions + optional timezone)
- Scheduled tasks reuse the same message + push-notification schema as one-off tasks

### Proposal
New RPC methods under the tasks/schedule/* namespace:

- tasks/schedule/create
- tasks/schedule/list
- tasks/schedule/update 
- tasks/schedule/delete

### Cron-like schedule:

- A required cron string (5- or 6-field, standard notation)
- Optional timezone (IANA name, defaults to UTC)
- Optional startTime / endTime if clients want to bound a schedule

### Payload:

Exactly the same TaskSendParams (id, sessionId, message, pushNotification, historyLength, metadata)

Server will materialize a new A2A task/send at each firing

author: jmandel
title: Is "history[]" really a "state transition history"?
raw_text:
It looks like there may be two competing models of history:

> stateTransitionHistory?: boolean; //true if the agent exposes status change history for tasks

> optional history of status and Messages.

The text in both spots suggests that historical statuses will be available... but the data model says:

>   history?: Message[];

(And this matches what's in the [json defs](https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/e2d6addfc6c8fe18501f0130a668427d0ae92d70/specification/json/a2a.json#L1556-L1567))

In other words, the history structure has messages but not statuses.

It would be good for history to look more like an actual status history, meaning an array of items like:

```
{
  state,
  timestamp,
  message[]
}
```

author: jmandel
title: Does "history" include the current message (if any)?
raw_text:
>[Get a Task](https://google.github.io/A2A/#/documentation?id=get-a-task)
Clients may use this method to retrieve the generated Artifacts for a Task. The agent determines the retention window for Tasks previously submitted to it. An agent may return an error code for Tasks that were past the retention window for an agent or for Tasks that are short-lived and not persisted by the agent.
>The client may also request the last N items of history of the Task which will include all Messages, in order, sent by client and server. By default this is 0 (no history).

When the current task status includes a "message", does that same message also appear in the final element of "history[]"? It would be helpful to specify either way.

author: aickin
title: One of the messages in the spec examples is missing a role
raw_text: In the section of the spec on [multi-turn conversations](https://google.github.io/A2A/#/documentation?id=multi-turn-conversations), the response message should have `role: "agent"` but omits the role completely. I believe this violates the schema of Message.

author: aickin
title: Client IDs can get hijacked by other clients in tasks/send if they are not globally unique
raw_text:
In the tasks/send method, the client creates a ID for the task, which is used throughout the rest of the API to identify the task. This seems dangerous to me, as the client may create an ID that is not globally unique, and this opens up the possibility that another client could hijack the ID.

It is of course true that clients can use crypto libraries or uuid libraries to generate IDs that are probably globally unique, but that seems like a pretty heavy burden for clients. Why not remove ID from SendTaskParams and have the remote agent generate an ID that it is sure is unique, and which the remote agent returns in the response?

author: dhdanie
title: Multiple session IDs in a single task

author: darkhaniop
title: Split common and agent-specific dependencies
raw_text:
## Problem

Currently, if we want to use `common` modules, `A2AServer` and/or `common.types`, by installing `samples/python` in a new project, all the dependencies for `agents/google_adk`, `agents/langgraph`, and hosts are also installed. **Which adds ~90-100 packages to the dependency tree of the new project, while modules likely to be used in other projects (`common`+`utils`) require only 24 relatively small packages.**

## Proposed solution

I propose splitting "common" package requirements from those of agent and client examples. This can be done with minimal changes to the current code base by moving dependencies to `pyproject.toml` definitions in the respective sub-projects, e.g., `agents/langgraph/pyproject.toml`.

Doing this would:
* help reduce bloating dependency trees of new agent projects (both in a2a-samples and external);
* and help establish better patterns for adding new agent examples to this repository.

## Relevant issues and PRs

* [PR #149](https://github.com/google/A2A/pull/149#pullrequestreview-2769116396)

author: cdavernas
title: How to document multiple agents in `.well-known/agent.json` on a single host?
raw_text:
Hi A2A team,

I'm wondering how best to document multiple agents served from the same host using the `.well-known/agent.json` discovery mechanism.

For example, suppose a server hosts several agents with different models or backing providers — should each agent have its own `agent.json` exposed under a unique path, or is there a supported way to describe multiple agents in a single `.well-known/agent.json`?

If there's an emerging convention or recommendation for this scenario, I’d love to align with it.

Thanks!

author: jmandel
title: How does a server respond to sse request for a "completed" task
raw_text:
> Agents must set final: true attribute at the end of the stream or if the agent is interrupted and require additional user input.

If a task is in a completed state and a client re-subscribes, what should the response be? I could imagine...

1. Content-Type: text/event-stream with an empty body (but this violates the "must set final: true" language above)
2. Content-Type: text/event-stream and a single event that... has nothing but `"final": true` in its `result` object
3. Error response?
4. Something else?

This is important because clients won't always know whether a task is in a completed status before calling resubscribe (e.g.  there are race conditions, and also the state machine does not strictly prohibit transitions away from completed statuses).

My client was considering "closed SSE response with no events" to be an error condition and was resubscribing automatically (I don't think that's behavior we want, and it would be good if the spec made this explicit).

The title for the TaskIdParams section should be `"title": "TaskIdParams"` but it is currently listed as `"title": "TaskQueryParams"`

https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/cd9eb5e66219e4afab4e35985b2514bda5943b1f/specification/json/a2a.json#L1669

author: rogpeppe
title: specification/json: discriminator fields should probably be mandatory not optional
raw_text:
Currently various parts of the JSON Schema use the `anyOf` keyword with alternatives that don't provide a reliable way of distinguishing between them.

For example, the [`Part` definition](https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/cd9eb5e66219e4afab4e35985b2514bda5943b1f/specification/json/a2a.json#L1222-L1233) has three possibilities, [`DataPart`](https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/cd9eb5e66219e4afab4e35985b2514bda5943b1f/specification/json/a2a.json#L505), [`FilePart`](https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/cd9eb5e66219e4afab4e35985b2514bda5943b1f/specification/json/a2a.json#L597), and [`TextPart`](https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/cd9eb5e66219e4afab4e35985b2514bda5943b1f/specification/json/a2a.json#L1913), each of which are distinguished by the `type` property with const values `data`, `file`, and `text` respectively. However, in none of those cases is the `type` field mandatory, which means that that it's entirely possible for a single object to satisfy multiple possible arms of the `anyOf` (for example by providing both `data` and `text` fields, something that's technically allowed by the schema).

I suggest that it's better practice to make such discriminator fields required rather than optional.

A few other examples of places where discrimination isn't clear:
- [SendTaskStreamingResponse.result](https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/cd9eb5e66219e4afab4e35985b2514bda5943b1f/specification/json/a2a.json#L1385-L1395)
- various places, mostly error types, which use the idiom `"anyOf": [{}, {"type": "null"}]` which is technically a no-op because `{}` also admits `null`, so this is equivalent to omitting the `anyOf` entirely.

Tightening this up would help clients that use the JSON Schema as a source of truth for automatically generated code in other languages, allowing the code to reliably choose a concrete type given a schema-conforming JSON value.

I'm happy to provide a PR if this is deemed a reasonable change.

author: SecretiveShell
title: Support relative URLs in agent.json card
raw_text:
I have implemented an agent card in a python FastAPI application at the `.well-known/agent.json`. And I can read that correctly in the example CLI application.

If I set the URL to be a relative path inside of the agent card then I get the following error:
```python
  File "C:\Users\<name>\Documents\A2A\samples\python\.venv\Lib\site-packages\httpx\_transports\default.py", line 118, in map_httpcore_exceptions
    raise mapped_exc(message) from exc
httpx.UnsupportedProtocol: Request URL is missing an 'http://' or 'https://' protocol.
```

As it stands right now, I would have to add extra configuration logic to my application to template the URL into the agent card response. I cannot just hardcode localhost or example.com since it changes across dev/production. Since the client already needs to know the domain name to fetch the agent card in the first place this seems redundant.

It should be possible to map `http://localhost:8000/.well-known/agent.json` with a url `/a2a/agent` to `http://localhost:8000/a2a/agent.json`

The only issue I see with this feature would be that the [Curated Discovery (Registry-Based)](https://google.github.io/A2A/#/topics/agent_discovery?id=curated-discovery-registry-based) would become harder, though this can be solved by having the registry service resolve the URL at the time it is added to the registry.

author: kdenhartog
title: A2A should account for the confused deputy problem
raw_text:
I've originally authored an issue in MCP describing this problem [here](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/issues/333).

I think A2A should also consider the impact of the issue and account for it. For context here's a copy of the issue over there:

>This issue is to discuss the handling of the [confused deputy problem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Confused_deputy_problem) and determine what makes the most sense for handling this.
> 
> Under the current design being discussed in https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/issues/205 and the subsequent PR https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/pull/284 there's a simplified assumption being made of the MCP client. In the case of most agents, they won't be granted the full set of authorization scopes typically granted to the user's client (aka the browser). Instead, we should expect that it will be common that an agent will be granted a subset of authorization scopes specifically for performing a task.
> 
> In this case, an agent (or it's child-agents) will perform separated tasks on behalf of the user and if we re-use the token that's intended for the client it will produce a confused deputy problem. To address this issue, the resource server needs to be able to identify the client and it's agents as independent entities with separated authorization scopes in order to adhere with the [principle of least privilege](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Principle_of_least_privilege).
>
>---
>
>There are two ways that I can think of solving this:
>
>
> ### 1. child-agent issuance protocol
>
>In this design choice, a client/agent submits a token to the authorization server to get issued a secondary token it can grant to the child-agent to operate on its own behalf. The agent/child-agent would then present this token to the resource server.
>
> ### 2. Delegation chains using DPoP
>
> In this design choice, we instead rely on delegation chains where a client can take a subject key from a DPoP architecture (such as [DBSC](https://github.com/w3c/webappsec-dbsc)) and issue a secondary token with a subset of the authorization scopes. Then, when the agent/child-agent submits a request to the resource server (MCP server), the resource server would validate the delegation chain by verifying the token issued to the client and the token issued to the agent by the client's DPoP subject key.
>
> ---
>
> My personal preference is towards option 2 as it produces a more scalable approach by reducing the amount of requests necessary. Additionally, it allows for the ability of agents to spawn child-agents and handle authorization without coordination with the authorization server each time this occurs. However, this does rely on further changes to clients and the resource server (MCP server) in order to handle this which aren't as simple as option 1.
> 
> Luckily we're pretty well heading in that direction already with [DBSC](https://github.com/w3c/webappsec-dbsc) in the client, and would just need to add a delegate functionality to make this work. On the resource server side though, we'd also need to handle the more advanced verification logic and that's what I'd like to propose as an enhancement to this spec.

author: leavez
title: Can Structured Query task replace MCP tool call?
raw_text:
Currently, task requests are expressed in plain text, which serves the primary design goal of enabling agents to talk to each other.
```
"parts": [{
        "type":"text",
        "text": "Show me a list of my open IT tickets",
...
```

I wonder is it possible to give the core query as a structured data (like below). And the remote agent implementing A2A is not a real agent backed by a LLM, instead, is a plain normal http server implementing tool calls (like a mcp server)

```
"parts": [{
        "type":"text",
        "metadata": {
            "tool": "get tickets",
            "args: "{\"owner_id\": 123}"
        }
...
```
( `metadata` is referenced from https://google.github.io/A2A/#/documentation?id=structured-output)

**So why doing this?** 
- A2A is more friendly for long running tasks. MCP has no guarantee for long running task when using http transport. For example, A 10 mins sync request is terrible. MCP also doesn't support streaming the result.
- Unify tools and agents communication in one protocol. This is convenient for dev and maintaining. Also, putting everything together may spark more creativity or interesting interactions.

author: mting806
title: Suggestion: add message queue for the transport
raw_text:
Benefits:
- Asynchronous communication – decouples sender and receiver
- Built-in rate control – buffer messages and control processing speed
- Better fault tolerance – messages are not lost if consumer is down
- Scalable – easy to add more consumers to handle load
- Improves system resilience – smooths traffic spikes (e.g., burst handling)
- Flexible architecture – enables event-driven or microservices patterns

author: patwhite
title: Need Guidance on Notification Message Security OR Negotiation Protocol
raw_text:
The guidance on notification security is overly broad - the protocol should either have very specific guidance, ie directly specifying a notification security mechanism, OR should have a clear mechanism for negotiating / signaling a notification security protocol (in the same way you have the general auth mechanism field ["bearer"]

> [Asymmetric keys](https://google.github.io/A2A/#/topics/push_notifications?id=asymmetric-keys)
> A pair of private and public keys can be generated using ECDSA, RSA etc. These can be generated by the notification server or the remote agent.
> 
> If the key pair is generated by the notification server, (ex. APNS), the private key needs to be supplied to the agent. The notification server should keep the public key to verify incoming request payloads signed by the agent using the private key.
> If the key pair is generated by the agent. Then there can be two options:
> The public key is manually provided to the Notification Receiver.
> Or the public keys can be provided by the agent through JWKS protocol.
> Agents can sign request payload using the private key and provide the request signature as a header. Or they can use JWT protocol to generate a token and provide that as a signature. Benefit of JWT protocol would also be that it standardises common fields like keyId, request timestamp.
> 
> [Symmetric keys](https://google.github.io/A2A/#/topics/push_notifications?id=symmetric-keys)
> A simpler method can be that both notification server and agents use the same shared key to sign and verify. The notification server verifies the signature by re-signing the payload with the key. Again JWT can be used to generate the signature token.
> 
> Asymmetric keys have an advantage as only the agent knows the public key and hence less chances of the key being leaked.

author: patwhite
title: MCP Has a Convention for Subscribe, should stick with that

author: patwhite
title: Task Push Notifications Get Needs Implementation Details
raw_text:
Task Push Notifications Get, as written, is unclear. I think we're going for some sort of polling based event retrieval, but this isn't a great model for polling based events, it should include an ack of the get if that's the case. Basically, in implementation here, you would tend toward web hook style notifications where you retry for a certain number of times, then either halt your event stream (if it's in critical that events are processed in order) or dead letter the webhook call and move on.

The Get model here should work more like pubsub where an event is taken for processing then has to be acked to clear it off a topic queue.

author: kedoska
title: Feature Request: Support for Personality Injection at Orchestrator Level
raw_text:
## Background

In our current implementation of the A2A protocol, we found it necessary to inject **personality information** from the **orchestrator layer** rather than baking it into the agent directly. This approach is driven by the requirement to support **multi-brand and multi-market** deployments, where the same agent logic might serve different audiences with unique tone and behavior expectations.


## Request
Extend the A2A protocol to support **structured personality injection**, enabling:

- **Dynamic definition of tone of voice** (e.g., friendly, formal, playful)
- **Optional behavioral traits** (e.g., concise vs. detailed responses)
- **Few-shot exemplars** correlated with the personality to guide responses more effectively

### Suggested Approach

- Extend the protocol schema to allow for an optional `personality` object in the `AgentRequest`.
- The `personality` object could include:
  - `tone`: A string or enum to define the tone (e.g., "friendly", "professional", "witty")
  - `traits`: A key-value map or list of specific traits (e.g., `verbosity: "concise"`)
  - `few_shots`: An optional list of example interactions to illustrate the desired personality

## Examples of usage

### 🧑‍💼 Professional (en-GB)
```yaml
personality:
  locale: en-GB
  tone: formal
  traits:
    verbosity: detailed
    humour: none
  few_shots:
    - user: "Can you help me place an order?"
      assistant: "Certainly. Please follow the link I’ve provided to begin placing your order. Should you encounter any issues, I’m here to assist further."
    - user: "What’s your return policy?"
      assistant: "Our return policy allows returns within 30 days of receipt. Items must be unused and in original packaging."
```

### 😎 Casual (en-US)
```yaml
personality:
  locale: en-US
  tone: friendly
  traits:
    verbosity: medium
    humour: light
  few_shots:
    - user: "Can you help me place an order?"
      assistant: "Of course! Just hit the link and you’re good to go. If anything’s confusing, I got you!"
    - user: "What’s your return policy?"
      assistant: "You’ve got 30 days to return it—just make sure it’s unused and still in the box!"
```

### 🎉 Playful (en-US)
```yaml
personality:
  locale: en-US
  tone: playful
  traits:
    verbosity: short
    humour: high
  few_shots:
    - user: "Can you help me place an order?"
      assistant: "Absolutely! Let’s make some coffee magic ☕✨—click this link and boom, you’re all set."
    - user: "What’s your return policy?"
      assistant: "Changed your mind? No worries! 30 days to send it back—as long as it’s still lookin’ fresh."
```



Thanks for the great work on A2A!

author: fwklucznik
title: Exploring role fidelity and orchestration logic compatibility with A2A
raw_text:
First off...thank you for publishing this. Saw it on LinkedIn. A2A fills a real gap in the agent ecosystem, and the clarity of the protocol is impressive.

I’ve built a multi-agent orchestration system that’s already functioning as a live cognitive environment...with distinct roles, persistent memory, and internal coordination logic. The core focus is on orchestration, not just communication. Agents operate as a team, with structured handoffs, emergent behavior, and system-level reflection. Would love to connect the two teams if it makes sense.

I’m curious whether the A2A roadmap includes:
   - Support for persistent agent identity and role fidelity across sessions or ecosystems
   - Patterns for cross-agent memory references or multi-turn handoffs
   - Extensions for team-based logic orchestration, beyond bilateral messaging

Not looking to pitch...just genuinely exploring alignment points. If there’s additional architectural guidance beyond what’s in the repo, I’d be grateful for a pointer.

Thanks again for putting this into the open. It’s a big step forward

author: yu-iskw
title: Delegated User Authorization for Agent2Agent Servers
raw_text:
## Summary
We propose to enhance the Agent2Agent (A2A) ecosystem by integrating a delegated authentication and authorization mechanism—modeled after approaches like Composio AgentAuth and standard OAuth 2.0—to allow agents to securely act on behalf of users. This middleware enhancement will enable an Agent2Agent server (e.g., an MCP server) to delegate permissions and perform actions only with explicit user consent.

## Background
- **Need for Delegated Authorization:**  
  In multi-agent systems such as those utilizing the Model Context Protocol (MCP), it is essential that any agent performing actions on a user's behalf is explicitly authorized. Delegated user authorization helps mitigate the significant security risks when a server performs actions without verifying user permissions.

- **Current Limitations:**  
  While the Agent2Agent specification offers robust mechanisms for agent-to-agent communication, it does not natively implement an advanced delegated authentication mechanism similar to Composio AgentAuth. Instead, it relies on integration with external Identity Provider (IdP) systems (e.g., OAuth 2.0) to authenticate clients.

- **Community Discussions:**  
  The MCP community has been actively discussing these issues to define a proper specification for secure token delegation:  
  - [MCP Security Discussion – Issue #205](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/issues/205)  
  - [MCP Proposed Enhancements – PR #284](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/pull/284/files)

- **Enterprise Authentication Guidelines:**  
  Google's A2A documentation on authenticating clients in enterprise environments also provides guidance on securing agent interactions, which can serve as a valuable reference for this enhancement:  
  - [A2A Enterprise-Ready Authentication](https://google.github.io/A2A/#/topics/enterprise_ready?id=authenticating-clients)

## Proposed Enhancement
We propose the development of a middleware extension for Agent2Agent servers that enables delegated user authorization by integrating with external IdP platforms. The key components of this proposal include:

### 1. External IdP Integration
- **Objective:**  
  Integrate with external Identity Provider platforms (e.g., OAuth 2.0) to obtain and validate user-specific access tokens.  
  **Note:** While Agent2Agent does not currently support a mechanism like Composio AgentAuth directly, using it as a model can help illustrate the desired behavior.
  
- **Implementation:**  
  - Modify agent-to-agent requests to include an optional authentication header or token field.
  - Ensure these tokens are obtained from a trusted IdP and are validated on each request.

### 2. Delegated Authorization Mechanism
- **Objective:**  
  Enable agents to operate under the explicit permissions of a user.
  
- **Implementation:**  
  - Extend the A2A messaging schema with support for delegated token propagation.
  - Establish middleware to intercept and validate tokens, ensuring that every action performed by an agent is carried out on behalf of an authenticated user.
  - Leverage ideas from Composio AgentAuth to illustrate a secure flow of authentication data and token propagation.

### 3. Security and Auditability
- **Objective:**  
  Secure the authorization process and ensure transparency for all delegated actions.
  
- **Implementation:**  
  - Log all authentication and token validation events.
  - Provide mechanisms for token revocation and generate audit trails for any actions carried out using delegated permissions.
  - Utilize standard security practices to verify token integrity and expiration.

### 4. Backward Compatibility and Configurability
- **Objective:**  
  Ensure that existing Agent2Agent deployments are not disrupted.
  
- **Implementation:**  
  - Offer the delegated authorization feature as an optional module or configurable setting.
  - Maintain backward compatibility by allowing agents that do not require this feature to continue operating as usual.

## Benefits
- **Enhanced Security:**  
  Agents can only act with explicit user consent, mitigating risks associated with unauthorized actions.

- **Interoperability:**  
  Standardizing on external IdP integrations (using OAuth 2.0/Composio AgentAuth as a model) will provide a consistent, secure method for delegated authorization across diverse systems.

- **Future-Proofing:**  
  This enhancement aligns with the evolving security requirements for multi-agent systems, ensuring robust authentication and authorization in complex environments such as MCP.

## References
- [A2A Enterprise-Ready Authentication Guidelines](https://google.github.io/A2A/#/topics/enterprise_ready?id=authenticating-clients)
- [MCP Security Discussion – Issue #205](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/issues/205)
- [MCP Proposed Enhancements – PR #284](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/pull/284/files)
- [Composio AgentAuth Overview](https://composio.dev/agentauth/)

## Conclusion
By integrating an external IdP-based delegated authorization mechanism into Agent2Agent servers, we can ensure that agents operate strictly under the permissions granted by users. This feature will significantly enhance security in multi-agent systems such as MCP servers, making it easier and safer for agents to act on behalf of users.

author: xiongwenxin123
raw_text:
Points of Conflict with the Existing Protocol
Client vs. Remote Separation:

Current Design: The protocol strictly separates the roles of client and remote to maintain clear responsibilities and simplify communication.
Conflict: Allowing agents to act as both client and server simultaneously introduces ambiguity in role management and increases complexity.
Task Routing and Coordination:

Current Design: The client explicitly initiates and coordinates all tasks, with remotes acting as passive responders.
Conflict: In a cascading or mesh topology, task routing becomes decentralized, requiring a new protocol layer to manage task delegation and tracking.
Communication Protocol:

Current Design: Assumes a straightforward request-response model between client and remote.
Conflict: Cascading and mesh modes require support for multi-hop communication, state persistence across agents, and possibly asynchronous task handling.
Performance Overhead:

Current Design: Optimized for direct client-remote interactions with minimal latency.
Conflict: Cascading tasks or mesh communication can introduce significant latency and require careful optimization to prevent bottlenecks.
State Management:

Current Design: Agents only maintain state relevant to their specific role (client or remote).
Conflict: Dual-role agents need mechanisms to synchronize state across roles, increasing the likelihood of inconsistencies.

author: DGuhr
raw_text:
@hmk I personally think these are very valid and important questions. Disclaimer: I'm not building a product or something in this area, but coming from the IAM space, this is a very interesting topic. I already wrote a comment on the [MCP](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/issues/205#issuecomment-2781082042 ) issue here. 

In short: I don't have the answer, but I think it's fair to assume OAuth is not enough, and I like that A2A distinguishes between user and client authorization (at least as of my understanding of the [enterprise ready](https://google.github.io/A2A/#/topics/enterprise_ready) part I've read). 

My current, most likely false mental model is around something like I've scribbled in the picture below. This doesn't mean by all means "do it like this", it's meant as a "cobbled together in 30min" start to explore more valid things together with my colleagues, so please don't roast me for this ;-) All models are wrong, but some are useful. I hope this is the latter. If not, please enlighten me ;)

Also, it was made before I knew of A2A, so there's a bit to change for sure. For example, when I understand it correctly, mTLS is a thing now, and maybe also things like DPoP and/or [HTTP Message Signatures](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9421.html) might be used. 

I also hope newer ABAC / ReBAC policy engines will be taken into account (e.g. OpenID authZEN, though I don't like their XACML-Approach, but we need fine grained user authz, not just OAuth with coarse grained entitlements in tokens), together with a lifecycle, where you can see an example in the picture. 

I have more thoughts and would love to explore this with likeminded people, so I'm happy to be told "that's all wrong", but then please point me to the right direction. I don't want uncontrolled goblins called agents do things without proper authZ/authN/auditability ;) 

**edit:** jftr: the intent-checker in the picture could be some kind of policy engine or even its own small LLM that checks against heuristics (ML Classifiers and so on). I won't link on company-specific closed source products, and don't know of an OSS one, but this is already in the making.

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/878c0ea7-eb20-4f3c-b4b2-98e84abd8219)

author: alexbainbridge
raw_text:
End of 2024 I trialled adding service endpoint descriptions (using OpenAI style tool function definitions of the time) into a DID Document (already mostly a W3C standard). This enables very mature user auth to take place for AI ready API endpoints. 

Here is an example (view DID Document on this page so you can see the service endpoints, and the AI function definitions) https://dev.uniresolver.io/#did:web:did.autoura.me:api:did:profile:eEJ6QzZpVUZSVWhmZ3A2Y1Y4UnAwREdkZkJrcytRZUIxZ2VERkdTVWhMeEZmYVVkNnQxRnhYNjAwOGxjZGMzVm5tU1EwQStxdkNq..:..QXlWRG1XcTM1RHp3MHExTUluN1B3VkNOaXViRkdTZkVKTWs2emtjczhXQkI0TnZDMmFIM0VxNEtKSTZNQVF3SzR4MU9uY2EyUFQ0..:..RmpHRk9iZHNsMy9od2JSSnBUQ2QzZFNZNTdKNE54UWdKSzdUWU5KK3Qx

IF DIDs can be handled within A2A then that would be a good path forward. Many next gen identity solutions support DIDs.

https://www.w3.org/TR/did-1.0/

3. .venv/Scripts/activate.ps1

author: kthota-g
raw_text:
A task's completion can be identified by `task.status.state == 'completed'`. Artifacts are the end result of a task and messages are used for communicating updates that require additional user input, status updates, errors etc.

In the above example, since the agent requires additional user inputs to complete the task, the updates are sent as a message with state `input-required`

author: kthota-g
raw_text:
Agents can have memory that help manage the conversion history in multi-turn scenarios. Task's sessionId being sent by client can be used as the thread identifier for such conversations.

If an agent doesn't have memory, the conversation history needs to be sent to the agent to set the context.

author: darkhaniop
raw_text:
I have a question about this patch. In this implementation, `get_api_key()` loops through known API keys in a predetermined order. Which means, if we have more than one key in our env, we cannot make the LangGraph agent pick the one that comes later in the `SUPPORTED_API_KEYS` list.

Then, let's say someone tries to launch multiple agent servers as described in [README](https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/8c8e0eabfe0558159044455e917b38c7472a9fe1/samples/python/README.md), `ReimburementAgent` (setting `GOOGLE_API_KEY`) and the LangGraph-based one (e.g., with `OPENAI_API_KEY`):

1. `cd samples/python` (where they have their `.env`).
2. `uv run agents/google_adk` *and* `uv run agents/langgraph`

Both of the agents would still use `GOOGLE_API_KEY`.

Maybe if the script has access to more than one key, it can be updated to require specifying the provider to use? Or provide other ways of choosing the provider?

author: patwhite
raw_text:
"A2A is focused on a different problem. A2A is an application level protocol that enables agents to collaborate in their natural modalities. It allows agents to communicate as agents (or as users) instead of as tools. We hope that A2A gains adoption as a complement to MCP that enables ecosystems of agents and will be working in the open with the community to make this happen."

I think you guys should rethink this slightly to consider MCP as more of a base protocol that this builds upon. MCP has already figured out things like session negotiation, session resumption, a bunch of stuff really missing in detail from the A2A spec.

> 
> Then, let's say someone tries to launch multiple agent servers as described in [README](https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/8c8e0eabfe0558159044455e917b38c7472a9fe1/samples/python/README.md), `ReimburementAgent` (setting `GOOGLE_API_KEY`) and the LangGraph-based one (e.g., with `OPENAI_API_KEY`):

+1 to what you said. Every agent should read all the env variables because env variables are set at the system level and all running process should have access to it.

What we can do is have each agent define what keys they are going to use and perform validation at the agent level if any of the required env vars which the agent needs to use is not set.

author: zhenjunMa
raw_text:
There are three agent in this project, and they use the different port to provide service.
```
langgraph -> 10000
crewai -> 10001
adk -> 10002
```
The client connects to different agents through different port. By default, it uses port 10000, which is for the LangGraph agent. If you want to connect to other agents, you can modify the target address through the startup parameters. The way you've changed it now has caused a port conflict between CrawAI and LangGraph.

author: hotdogarea
raw_text:
I am very optimistic about the future of A2A, so I am actively learning about it.
In the demo, we know that there are three kinds of agents developed by different frameworks, which can be developed by different vendors. They can't work directly with each other on their own. In this case, the host agent must go through for collaboration. And the message must be forwarded by the host agent. It doesn't count as direct communication.
Another scenario is that if both agents are developed based on the A2A protocol, whether the host agent is required in this scenario. I don't know if my understanding is rigorous or not, but I'm still learning, thanks to the efforts and contributions of the google team

author: uttej-vsk
raw_text: I think, the negotiation part of the protocol handles that. In many cases, the client doesn't need to know about individual skills at all—it only cares about the final output. The responsibility of figuring out which skills to invoke, how to compose them, and how to execute the task should ideally be delegated to the server-side agent. Not sure the intent behind the issue but it would be helpful if you could elaborate on this, so that anyone who sees this question, would understand the "Why" behind this. I'm curious to learn about your usecase, though.

author: vladislavdonchev
raw_text:
**Full Changelog / Summary of Non-KG Changes:**

*   **Type Hinting & Model Definitions:**
    *   Standardized the use of `Optional[X]` instead of `Union[X, None]` for optional fields across Pydantic models for consistency (e.g., `Optional[str]`, `Optional[List[...]]`).
    *   Added `description` attributes to many Pydantic model fields to improve clarity and auto-generate better schema descriptions.
    *   Ensured list fields like `Task.artifacts` and `AgentCapabilities.knowledgeGraphQueryLanguages` use `default_factory=list` to initialize as empty lists rather than `None` by default.
    *   Refined the `Part` type definition using `Annotated` and `Field(discriminator="type")` for better type discrimination.
    *   Removed extraneous/incorrect fields that might have been added during development (e.g., `final` from `TaskArtifactUpdateEvent`, `stream` from `TaskSendParams`).

*   **JSON Schema (`a2a.json` - Implied Updates):**
    *   Incorporated field descriptions from Pydantic models.
    *   Added format specifiers (`"format": "uri"`, `"format": "date-time"`, `"format": "uuid"`) where applicable for better schema validation and documentation.
    *   Added content encoding hint (`"contentEncoding": "base64"`) for `FileContent.bytes`.
    *   Added discriminators where appropriate (`Part`, `A2ARequest`) for clearer schema definition.
    *   (Note: The handling of errors requiring `data: null` was addressed by changing the *test serialization logic*, not the schema itself, to ensure compliance.)

*   **Error Handling & Validation:**
    *   Improved error handling in `A2AServer._process_request` to catch more specific exceptions (`json.JSONDecodeError`, `pydantic.ValidationError`, `MethodNotFoundError`, `NotImplementedError`) before the generic `Exception` handler.
    *   Mapped specific exceptions to appropriate JSON-RPC error responses and HTTP status codes (e.g., 400 for validation/parse errors, 404 for MethodNotFound, 501 for NotImplemented, 500 for InternalError).
    *   Added `ContentTypeNotSupportedError` to the suite of defined errors and tests.
    *   Corrected the schema validation logic in `test_a2a_spec.py` for specific error types (`MethodNotFoundError`, `TaskNotFoundError`, etc.) that require the `"data": null` field to be present in the JSON output, by conditionally adjusting `model_dump` parameters (`exclude_none=False`) during testing.

*   **Server & Client Logic:**
    *   Added capability checks in `A2AServer` to return `MethodNotFoundError` (and HTTP 404) if a requested method (like streaming or push notifications) is not supported according to the `AgentCard`.
    *   Added corresponding capability checks in `A2AClient` to raise `NotImplementedError` client-side if attempting to use an unsupported feature.
    *   Improved HTTP error reporting in `A2AClient` to include response body details when available.
    *   Enhanced `A2AClient` streaming methods (`send_task_streaming`, `knowledge_subscribe`) to handle potential errors during SSE event processing and yield error responses within the stream.

*   **`InMemoryTaskManager` Improvements:**
    *   Refined task cancellation logic (`on_cancel_task`) to correctly handle different task states and notify subscribers.
    *   Improved `upsert_task` to handle history updates and task state transitions more robustly when messages are added to existing tasks.
    *   Added checks in push notification getters/setters to ensure the target task exists first.
    *   Refined SSE queue management (`setup_sse_consumer`, `enqueue_events_for_sse`, `dequeue_events_for_sse`), including handling of resubscriptions and queue cleanup.

*   **Testing (`test_a2a_spec.py`):**
    *   Adopted consistent use of `model_dump(mode='json', ...)` for generating test instances.
    *   Added tests covering more minimal/default cases for various types.
    *   Improved error reporting in the `validate_instance` helper.
    *   Explicitly included base RPC types in some tests for clarity.

author: jmandel
raw_text:
>  would understand the "Why" behind this. I'm curious to learn about your usecase, though.

If there are several skills each with different input requirements, one "why" is... I want to be certain the server understands which I am invoking. As a server developer this gives me a path to more deterministic logic and less guesswork for routing a task (it'd be silly to route it to the wrong handler in a case where the client *explicit intended* to invoke a specific skill, which they must have in order to provide the skill-specific input data.)

author: darkhaniop
raw_text:
Hi, earlier I also ran into a potential code duplication problem, but I managed to use these modules without duplicating them.

In case you want to reuse those modules in another repository, you can install them directly from GitHub with:

```bash
pip install git+https://github.com/google/A2A.git#subdirectory=samples/python
```

Unfortunately, due to the existing directory structure in `samples/python`, there are some caveats to this method.

### Details

While working on another example agent, I wanted to reuse the existing code from `common` (the things you mention above), but I could not use the structure similar to the three existing example agents (because of the naming conflict that I explained in a [comment](https://github.com/google/A2A/issues/51#issuecomment-2800157406) in google-a2a/a2a-samples#29). So, I developed my example in a separate repository, where I still used files from `samples/python/langgraph` as a boilerplate.

I read [`samples/python/pyproject.toml`](https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/72b60eb6e6f28284a1d7b3723f4f59057153e7c4/samples/python/pyproject.toml) and saw that it defines the `a2a-samples` package that includes "common" and "hosts" (luckily for my little project, it excludes the "agents" dir itself). Possibly this was done to make importing these modules from the three sample agents more convenient. The current project structure makes "common" and "hosts" top-level packages within environments they are used in (which I think is unfortunate), so maybe they did not consider making these modules to be easily reusable externally (maybe?).

### What about reorganizing the modules now?

I think initially making them subpackages within some less common name, e.g., `a2a_samples.common` and `a2a_samples.hosts` would have been more convenient for us. However, I am a little worried about the idea of "restructuring" the project now🤔 It could break some "in-flight" contributions. At the same time, being able to install them from PyPI and use them with something like `from a2a_samples.common import A2AServer` would have been more convenient. I wonder what others think..

---
P.S. When managing environments with uv, currently the "common" package (that contains `types` and `A2AServer`) can be added to the dependencies in `pyproject.toml` as follows:
```toml
dependencies = [
    ...
    "a2a-samples",
]

[tool.uv.sources]
a2a-samples = { git = "https://github.com/google/A2A.git", subdirectory = "samples/python" }
```

In `uv.lock` it will be pinned to a specific commit.

*Edit: grammar, clarity*

author: kdenhartog
raw_text:
> When one agent has a CVE open, it's extremely relevant for consumer agents to know about it.

Agreed, and I think the usage of delegation chains here makes this reasonable to account for rather autonomously.

For example, an agent could theoretically utilize the same revoke functionality that a client would use with an agent to revoke a sub-agents token based on a scan of it's sub-agents CVEs. Given it's possible to handle this by reusing the revoke logic, I'd suggest we build on this in a  separate issue. But to give you a high level idea of how I see this working it would be:

1. The client requests a token from the Authz server
2. The Authz server authenticates the user and issues a token to the client
3. The client issues a delegated token with it's DBSC subject key to a coordination agent that also scans CVEs
4. The coordination agent issues a delegated token utilizing it's subject key (contained in the token issued in step 3) to a sub-agent with a subset of authorization scopes IFF it doesn't detect a CVE
5. The sub-agent conducts it's tasks and responds to the coordination agent

If at some point in time the coordination agent detects a CVE, it can then revoke the token by notifying either the RS/AS (not sure which works better yet). Then, the next time the sub-agent submits a request to the RS, it will receive a 403 error.

author: kthota-g
raw_text:
Hi,
If you like the agent to be available for open discovery, the agent card should be made available at a different path or running on a different port for each agent. However, if you don't need open discovery for your agent, you can expose the agent card at a different url and allow the client to fetch the agent card. The example python client, accepts the agent-card url as a parameter -  https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/71b5a485a9cf844a531e8a8c821507d9358dd4b9/samples/python/common/client/card_resolver.py#L10

As documented here, https://google.github.io/A2A/#/topics/agent_discovery, we think enterprises scenarios may require an agent to fetch a list of entitled agents via some form of registry.

We are looking to provide more clarity on this requirement in the next revision of the spec. If you have any recommendations or suggestions, please feel free to share with us.

author: aickin
raw_text:
I agree with @jmandel here; I genuinely thought it was just a bug in the spec that you don't specify which skill you want to use in `task/send`.

On a practical level, I can absolutely imagine wanting to tell an agent in a more structured way than just instructions what I want it to do. As a simple example, I may want to tell a travel agent that I just want the `getPrice` skill to be used and not the `purchaseTicket` skill to make sure I don't accidentally spend money. While I could rely on the remote agent to understand the natural language instructions in messages, it's much better if the remote agent has deterministic code that only invokes payment when `purchaseTicket` is invoked and not when other skills are invoked.

If the position of the spec is that the remote agent always gets to decide which skills to apply, then the spec should at least say this explicitly imo. Cheers!

Doc string says

```typescript
/**
   * Retrieves the AgentCard.

But there are no implementations to retrieve an agent card from /.well-known/agent.json. Should it be a distinguished method in API or there are need to be a custom implementation of AgentCard? Any thoughts on that?
