author: darkhaniop
raw_text:
The tool used to generate `a2a.json` probably used the latest version [draft 2020-12](https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema). The key `definitions` was replaced with `$defs` in 2019-09 (predecessor of 2020-12).

From https://json-schema.org/draft/2019-09/draft-handrews-json-schema-validation-02#rfc.appendix.A
> "definitions"
>     Renamed to "$defs" to match "$ref" and be shorter to type. Schema vocabulary authors SHOULD NOT define a "definitions" keyword with different behavior in order to avoid invalidating schemas that still use the older name. While "definitions" is absent in the single-vocabulary meta-schemas referenced by this document, it remains present in the default meta-schema, and implementations SHOULD assume that "$defs" and "definitions" have the same behavior when that meta-schema is used. 

If that's the case, the only line that needs to be corrected is the `$schema` specifier from
https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/175cecd8dab1dd1d66d6c5b8b0ec7e5390a543d9/specification/json/a2a.json#L2
to:
```json
{
  "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema",
  ...
}
```

---
*Edit: add a quote from the referenced document*

author: zeroasterisk
raw_text:
I have read through the comments in this thread and the PR.

As of now, tasks are a part of a plan, which will be processed as needed, are durable for long running operations and are may not be processed immediately, but the processing of the plan is kind of outside of the scope of the A2A spec. 

"Hey do these things for me."

If we open ourselves up to task scheduling, we are opening ourselves to a much higher burden of implemention:

"Hey do these things for me, periodically, forever... And now with conditions, and now with end loop conditions, and now with capacity constraints, and do it N times staring X days from now, and shuffle your priorities this is most important, and, and, and..."

If you want to create an agent which exposes the skill of task enqueuing and management, I believe you can do so without this change to the protocol. As a client, you can schedule tasks prior to interacting with the remote agent. Your agent can build out the complexity above, or not. But the burden is on the implementer.

Sticking with the client/server analogy, I cannot do a scheduled POST, nor a POST scheduling multiple future responses, except from very specialized systems.

Is this feature so common that most or at least many agents will need it and will support it well?

author: HeetVekariya
raw_text:
### This issue is similar to #96 , so this is the temporary solution I find. Community knows about this issue, and work is going on this as stated [here](https://github.com/google/A2A/issues/96#issuecomment-2881883991).

Okay, Let me summarize everything.

**Problem** : 
- Demo UI is lagging so much, that users get experience of nothing happening in the background.
- Navigating between different pages takes way longer time.

**Cause** : 
- Polling is happening with very low duration of the time difference, i.e. 1 sec.

---

- UI works perfectly on `MacOS` as multiple Mac users confirmed that.
- **I tried `demo/ui` on two different systems, `Windows i5` and `Windows i7`** and found this temp fix for windows.

**Solution** : 
- Run the agent(s) before starting UI to avoid the connection error.
- Start UI.
- When the UI loads, i.e. you hit `http://localhost:12000`, first of all click to **`Disable`** button on the top right corner to disable the auto refresh and reduce the polling overhead. **Wait until the polling stops, you can it in the terminal.** (**NOTE** - If you navigate between pages using url, polling time will reset to default value which is 1 second, so navigate using the UI only.)
- Add agent(s) using agent tab. 
- Jump to `Conversation` page and start new conversation.
- UI automatically refreshes whenever user sends a query, so when user asks something, UI gets updated and it gets reflected in the UI.
- To see further steps, you need to manually refresh the page using the refresh button present at the top right corner. You will something like below, until the response is available and at the end response when it is available.

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/242314af-0e41-4315-bff5-438b394f93cc)

---

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/bad85f00-4079-4519-b8c2-f30dfc0ffe97)

author: darkhaniop
vuminhoang
raw_text:
@devilcoder01 @holtskinner 

I'm still encountering this issue (I open the repo on PyCharm on a Windows machine), but I've found a simple way to fix:
 - I deleted the current .env file in demo/ui/.env (this was a file I created using echo, or even by direct creation, and it still caused the error).
 - Then, I created a new .env file using Notepad, pasting in the necessary information.
 - When saving, I pressed “Save As”, selected  "All files" for the "Save as type" and set the encoding to “UTF-8” (you can find these two options in the save dialog).
 
Afterward, I moved that file into the demo/ui directory, and everything worked normally. The problem was likely with the file format; when writing directly, it was saved as UTF-16.

author: jland-redhat
raw_text:
> Hi @jland-redhat, this article may help to understand the differences between the Azure endpoint for OpenAI and the native one.
> 
> https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/switching-endpoints

Ok I missed the custom library.

So still have a few suggestions I would like to run by y'all.

There is a lot of commented code here which I am not in love with. 

What if instead we simplify the environment Variables. So instead of the current setup, what if we primarily stuck to `OPENAI_API_KEY` and `OPENAI_ENDPOINT`. (I see `AZURE_OPENAI_DEPLOYMENT_NAME` is being set to `deployment_name`, but it's unclear where this variable is actually used in the code.)

Including `OPENAI_ENDPOINT` also means that on  line 122 we could conditionally set that value if it exist. This would solve the issue for allowing us to connect to other LLMs that use the same specification as OpenAIChatCompletion such as the ones served through vLLM.


And for the Azure/Non-Azure Logic from what I can tell, the only code difference is on lines 137 and 155, where `service=AzureChatCompletion(` is called. We could introduce a single variable, like ENABLE_AZURE_OPENAI, and use it to conditionally leverage the Azure libraries in these two instances if it's set to True.

What are y'alls thoughts on that?

In the section describing the `AgentExtensions` object, added a note about individual API extensions being external to the core specification:

Hey @cdavernas , Thanks for the comment!

The schema defined in this PR is implemented to conform to the following RFC and Internet Draft.  
I’d appreciate it if you could also review this along with the upcoming PRs to `a2a-python`:

author: darkhaniop
raw_text:
The generated `AgentLinkTarget` schema definition does not include the `additionalProperties` property, without which link metadata, such as link `title`, cannot be supplied. Did it fail to pick up this declaration from `LinkTarget`?
https://github.com/google/A2A/blob/60ba67bc9a2ae82ee320424e6b2683334ebf4c3c/types/src/types.ts#L551-L555

By the way, I like the simplicity of this approach.

author: ognis1205
raw_text:
Hey @darkhaniop, thanks again for the comment and review!

I double-checked the JSON Schema spec, and it turns out that by default, all additional properties are allowed—meaning the schema will accept extra fields even without explicitly specifying `additionalProperties`:

https://json-schema.org/understanding-json-schema/reference/object#additionalproperties

It seems that the presence of `additionalProperties: {}` (which is equivalent to `additionalProperties: true`) in the `LinkTarget` schema—but not in `AgentLinkTarget`—is due to how `typescript-json-schema` generates the output:

https://github.com/ognis1205/A2A/blob/60ba67bc9a2ae82ee320424e6b2683334ebf4c3c/specification/json/a2a.json#L1139

That can definitely make the generated schema a bit confusing to read. That said, a couple of points to keep in mind:

 - When generating downstream code like `google/a2a-python`, we might need to ensure the appropriate options are set—even though there’s no issue with how things are currently handled within `google/A2A` itself.
 - From a schema validation perspective, the current setup is fine. However, when thinking in terms of SDK models, it would mean we don’t expose APIs to set fields like `title`, for instance (though this shouldn’t be a problem when parsing raw JSON).

What do you think? Let me know if I’m missing anything important.

P.S.
Many thanks to @Kevsy and @darrelmiller for the elegant simplicity of the schema.

author: darrelmiller
raw_text: As it was explained to me, for the streaming response, the first SSE event will either be a Message or a Task.  If the SSE event is a Message then there will be no more events.  If the SSE event is a Task then there may be more TaskStatusUpdateEvents or TaskArtifactUpdateEvents.

author: darrelmiller
raw_text:
~~This used to be possible with task/send because you could pre-populate the history of messages.~~

Correcting myself here.  You didn't used to be able to pass a history with task/send.  TaskSendParams and MessageSendParams only has a Message property.  However, in theory you could use a list of TextParts to simulate previous conversations.

author: ognis1205
raw_text:
Hey @codefromthecrypt, really appreciate your feedback!

## Relationship to HATEOAS

The mechanism of using `.well-known/api-catalog.json` to expose multiple Agent Card links and identify Agent endpoints carries a **hypermedia-driven, self-descriptive API structure**, which is conceptually close to HATEOAS. However, the current proposal addresses a different design context:

> "When multiple agents are hosted under a single base URL, how can we ensure standardized *Open Discovery*?"

In this context, client-side **state transitions** are not the primary concern. Instead, the focus is on the **discovery structure** of APIs and resources. Therefore, while conceptually adjacent, the **layer and intent** of this proposal differ from the original architectural principles of HATEOAS.

## Future Possibility

That said, if in the future we extend the specification such that the content of `.well-known/api-catalog.json` dynamically reflects:

- The client’s authentication or authorization context  
- The result of interactions with remote agents (e.g., session state, policy negotiation)

then the file could be considered as:

> "A discovery-layer application of the HATEOAS principle, where hypermedia dynamically reflects the client’s state."

However, I think it's more appropriate to consider these features as part of the API specification for the Agent Catalog / Registry.

While I'm not deeply versed in HATEOAS, I hope this perspective is on the right track.

For now, it’s still uncertain whether this feature request itself will be accepted by the community, so I think it might be better to prioritize getting the protocol evaluated by a broader audience—through PR demos including `google/a2a-python`—rather than pushing for this specific change. (The properties other than `href` aren’t critical at this point.)

That said, depending on how things develop, the points you raised could become important, so it’s definitely worth keeping them in mind for the future.

author: MengJiapeng
MengJiapeng
moonbox3
raw_text:
Hi @oliverlabs, great progress. I would really prefer to have a more explicit approach to configuring the chat service, versus relying solely on if X number of env vars configured. Something like this (doesn't have to be that involved, but the enum is nice): https://github.com/microsoft/semantic-kernel/blob/main/python/samples/concepts/setup/chat_completion_services.py

The method `get_chat_completion_service_and_request_settings` gives one entry point to get the service / settings. To make it easier, we don't need to include the request settings, we can have the method be something like `get_chat_completion_service` and have it return the service only. It's pretty easy then to even include other AI Services like Google, Mistral or Anthropic, as an example - for those who want to test with the specific service. 

We can also link to SK's docs on configuring chat completion services in the README so devs can understand what needs to be included, if there are ever questions: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/semantic-kernel/concepts/ai-services/chat-completion/?tabs=csharp-AzureOpenAI%2Cpython-AzureOpenAI%2Cjava-AzureOpenAI&pivots=programming-language-python#creating-a-chat-completion-service along with [this doc](https://github.com/microsoft/semantic-kernel/blob/main/python/samples/concepts/setup/ALL_SETTINGS.md#semantic-kernel-settings) that spells out what env vars are required.

author: mikeas1
ognis1205
raw_text:
## PoC: SDK Example for Agent Mounting and API Catalog Discovery

I’ve implemented a proof-of-concept SDK that demonstrates how to define and mount multiple A2A agents using `A2AStarletteRouteBuilder` and `A2AStarletteBuilder`.  Each agent is configured with its own `AgentCard`, skill set, and handler, and mounted to a shared Starlette application. The resulting server responds with a valid A2A catalog at `/.well-known/api-catalog.json`, like the following:

```json
{
  "linkset": [
    {
      "anchor": "http://localhost:9999/a2a/hello/",
      "describedby": [
        {
          "href": "http://localhost:9999/a2a/hello/agent.json",
          "type": "application/json"
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "anchor": "http://localhost:9999/a2a/echo/",
      "describedby": [
        {
          "href": "http://localhost:9999/a2a/echo/agent.json",
          "type": "application/json"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

### Minimal Example Code

```python
@click.command()
@click.option('--host', default='localhost')
@click.option('--port', default=9999)
def main(host: str, port: int):
    hello_skill = AgentSkill(
        id='hello_world',
        name='Returns hello world',
        description='just returns hello world',
        tags=['hello world'],
        examples=['hi', 'hello world'],
    )
    hello_card = AgentCard(
        name='Hello World Agent',
        description='Just a hello world agent',
        url=f'http://{host}:{port}/a2a/hello',
        version='1.0.0',
        defaultInputModes=['text'],
        defaultOutputModes=['text'],
        capabilities=AgentCapabilities(streaming=True),
        skills=[hello_skill],
        supportsAuthenticatedExtendedCard=False,
    )
    hello_handler = DefaultRequestHandler(
        agent_executor=HelloWorldAgentExecutor(),
        task_store=InMemoryTaskStore(),
    )
    hello_agent = A2AStarletteRouteBuilder(
        agent_card=hello_card,
        http_handler=hello_handler,
    )

    echo_skill = AgentSkill(
        id="echo",
        name="Echo input",
        description="Returns the input text as is",
        tags=["echo"],
        examples=["Hello!", "Repeat after me"],
    )
    echo_card = AgentCard(
        name="Echo Agent",
        description="An agent that echoes back your input.",
        url=f"http://{host}:{port}/a2a/echo",
        version="1.0.0",
        defaultInputModes=["text"],
        defaultOutputModes=["text"],
        capabilities=AgentCapabilities(streaming=True),
        skills=[echo_skill],
        supportsAuthenticatedExtendedCard=False,
    )
    echo_handler = DefaultRequestHandler(
        agent_executor=EchoAgentExecutor(),
        task_store=InMemoryTaskStore(),
    )
    echo_agent = A2AStarletteRouteBuilder(
        agent_card=echo_card,
        http_handler=echo_handler,
    )

    server = (
        A2AStarletteBuilder()
            .mount(hello_agent)
            .mount(echo_agent)
            .build()
    )
    uvicorn.run(server, host=host, port=port)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    try:
        main()
    except Exception:
        print(traceback.format_exc(), file=sys.stderr)
```

This can serve as a practical SDK example when reviewing this PR. The following is the related PoC implementation of the SDK:

- [google-a2a/a2a-python#109](https://github.com/google-a2a/a2a-python/pull/109) – SDK implementation

author: ognis1205
raw_text:
Hi A2A team, Thank you for the ongoing discussions and developments.

I’ve been following the conversation in [Discussion #166](https://github.com/google/A2A/discussions/166), where the idea of supporting multiple agents under a single base URL was explored. In that thread, the concept of introducing a `.well-known/api-catalog.json` endpoint to list available agents for Open Discovery was mentioned. Given this context, I’d like to better understand the current stance of the A2A maintainers on this approach. Specifically:

 - Do you see the introduction of an API Catalog at `.well-known/api-catalog.json` as a complementary enhancement to the existing `.well-known/agent.json` Open Discovery mechanism?
 - Are there any concerns or considerations regarding the adoption of such an API Catalog for discovering multiple agents under a single host?

Looking forward to your insights.

author: darkhaniop
raw_text:
@mikeas1, thank you very much for sharing another approach to this. From your message, it appears to me that your proposal addresses a broader range of concerns than mine (that overlaps with what I proposed above). I'm not sure if I fully understood some of the details in your approach. Therefore, I would like to try to summarize the differences between the two versions: mine (I'll use a shorter codename "**Ext-1.0**" below) and yours ("**Ext-2.0**") so that you and others can clarify if I've missed or misunderstood anything.

### Covered Scope

**Ext-1.0** enables the addition of API methods that may involve but do not necessarily imply processing by AI/LLMs. This:
* Enables alternative methods for sending requests to an agent, where the extension preprocesses the request before passing it to AI/LLMs.
* Allows interaction with the A2A agent's general or user-specific state.
* Implement other domain-specific APIs.

While items (5) and (6) on your list suggest **Ext-2.0** extensions can require modifying request/response format, I think you did not mean to limit extensions to such use cases. At least, it is unclear to me what it should cover and what its limitations might be. You may have intended to leave it vague intentionally to broaden the possible ways to extend the protocol?

### Invocation

**Ext-1.0** allows the declaration of custom RPC methods at the same endpoint that serves an A2A agent. Therefore, extension methods can be invoked with RPC calls. While I originally proposed adding custom RPC methods only, it can be modified to include alternative approaches as well. For example, allowing the declaration of custom HTTP GET/POST paths under the agent's base URL.

The **Ext-2.0** way states that the extension's docs would describe everything about the extension. This would likely cover Ext-1.0 invocation ways as well. Items (5) and (6) in your message suggest that (some) Ext-2.0 extensions can be invoked by the A2AServer when they are listed in the `extensions` prop of the requests. In this sense, they are more like server middleware (executed before and after the agent handler).

By the way, in what I proposed above, if we allow the re-declaration (replacement) of protocol-defined methods, Ext-1.0 extensions can also be used as middleware that preprocesses additional parameters before passing them to the agent handler. However, since the source code of the handler is available to the agent developers, they can integrate such middleware directly into their handlers (using the current version of the spec and SDK).

### Integration into A2A Servers and Clients

**Ext-1.0** proposes specific ways of adding custom APIs to A2A servers, enabling A2A libraries (e.g., the official A2A SDK) to implement a common logic for adding extension (e.g., `def add_extension(...)`). This allows for individual extension source code to be external to both A2A libraries and A2A agents. So, extensions can be easily shared and used as third-party dependencies.

**Ext-2.0** seems somewhat vague about this, which could lead to less compatible "extension adding functionality" being implemented in different A2A libraries (and lead to more developers opting to fork A2A libraries). And, going back to items (5) and (6), I feel that this approach is geared toward a case when an agent developer should have control over the extension source code (ie, maintain their own extensions). However, I may have misunderstood this part of the Ext-2.0 proposal.

### Extension-Specific Configuration

In **Ext-1.0**, I overlooked this aspect. The extension developers would need to develop their config set/get methods. Of course, Ext-1.0 does not define a standard, so it could become challenging to retrieve the whole configuration of an agent that uses many extensions.

**Ext-2.0**, on the other hand, offers a standardized way of retrieving extension configs through AgentCard. But I have questions about this feature.
* Does the `AgentExtension.params` prop include general or user-specific parameters? Or both?
* Can `params` be updated post-deployment? If yes (tying to the previous question), how can the client determine which of the parameters they are allowed to change?
* And following up on the previous one, if `params` are mutable, this means that any service that caches AgentCards (including A2A agents and registries) would have to set different TTLs for extensionful vs. extensionless agents. Or they would need to implement smarter caching logic. Maybe this is not a problem if, in the future, the A2A spec changes `.well-known/agent.json` to be dynamic (and all AgentCards are considered dynamic).

## Other

Please let me know if I've overlooked any other aspects or misunderstood something about your idea.

author: pstephengoogle
raw_text: If a Message is received the client should close the stream as no new events should be provided

author: pstephengoogle
raw_text:
A few answer:
Question 3:
That is up to the implementor. Both are valid (assuming the parts that are being merged are TextPart). Multiple Parts are intended to sequence ordering of different part types (e.g. text that refers to an image that is in the next part) so when it comes to sets of Text Parts, concatenation or multiple sequential text parts are equivalent. 

Question 4:
TaskStatusUpdate is the mechanism for streaming output support. If you wanted to stream content you would emit multiple update events. For example you could emit Update(message="Working on your request", status="working") or you could emit multiple events like {Update(message="Working", status="working"), Update(message="on", status="working"), Update(message="your", status="working"), Update(message="request", status="working")}. The protocol supports both, how you consume these and translate it into a user visible stream is up to you. The Task should contain the history of each of these messages too. When it comes to the user interaction, keep in mind that the goal of A2A is to allow other agent implementations to be consumed in applications/agents, so consider how others might produce the information (within the protocol spec) to have the best experience in general.

Also, when an agent pauses, the information that is relevant about the reason for the pause should be in the status message

Question 5:
Yes, the task history should include the full 'thread' of interactions between the client and server. So each status update event message should be in the history.

Question 6:
The protocol defines a state machine for task management. The two options for message/stream are:
- A single Message, this is then a completed stream, no additional events should be expected by the client
- A Task (in any state, doesn't have to be a task-submitted state) followed by any delta events on that task. If the initial Task isn't in a terminal or interrupted state (e.g. completed, failed, cancelled, input-required) then the delta events stream is incomplete until the final update is sent. If the initial Task is already in a terminal state, no update events are expected.

In short, the stream must be thought of as a series of mutation events until the Task is terminal/interrupted.

author: pstephengoogle
raw_text:
There were some updates to the spec in version 0.2.1 to try to be more precise. To summarize:

The Task lifecycle is based on a Task object followed by several TaskUpdateEvents (either StatusUpdate or ArtifactUpdates). A Task is updated on the server until a terminal or interrupted state is reached (completed, failed, cancelled or input-required). When such a state is reached, the stream is closed.

How the client interacts with server is a choice. There is support for streams, push and pull style interactions. The new `message/stream` is intended to support the streaming paradigm, where you receive a Task object followed by 0 to N update events, until the Task is in an interrupted/terminated state. The SSE connection is kept open until this is reached. The same behavior exists on the resubscribe call. This will re-establish the SSE connection and allow for streams to be consumed under the condition of a broken stream or a separate listener than the original requestor. A push paradigm is supported by registering a push notification callback for the task. This can be done at any point after the initial task creation. For the pull style behavior, one can use the `message/send` API and set the `configuration.blocking` field to `False`. The server should respond with the initial Task and this acts as a LRO container. The `task/get` can be called to pull the latest state of the task. Finally, the simplest interaction is to making a blocking `message/send` call (default behavior for this endpoint). The client will receive no signals until the Task is in a terminal/interrupted state.

For both `message/send` and `message/stream` the first payload (or only) is either a Message object or a Task object. If it is a message object, the interaction is completed (no task to track, no streaming events to follow). If it is a Task object, then the behavior of Tasks + updates as described above applies

author: pstephengoogle
raw_text:
Yes as Darrel states. The interaction with `message/send` and `message/stream` should always return EITHER a `Message` OR a `Task`. In the case of `message/send` those are the only options. For `message/stream` IF the first response is a `Task` AND the task is not in a terminal or interrupted state (completed, cancelled, failed or input-required) the stream should provide TaskStatusUpdateEvents and/or TaskArtifactUpdateEvents until a terminal/interrupted state (simplified to be detected by a final boolean on the update event). 

You can think of this as these endpoints produce either a
-`Message`; for one-off interactions that don't require complex lifecycle management, like answering a question about capability or clarifying the scope of work to accept
- `Task`; that contains the interactions and state of the Task at the time it is returned.

Streaming just provides the ability to observe, in real-time, all the deltas to the Task state as work is done.

author: pstephengoogle
raw_text:
It is possible to replace the host_agent.py implementation with one built on langgraph. The key to making this work is the task callback that is used when interacting with the remote agent to propagate the tasks information to the UI. If someone wants to implement an alternative version, please feel free to do so. We can add some sort of 'selector' to the demo UI app to choose which implementation to run

As for internal agent communication. It is done over http so any mechanism you have to tunnel to your local solution via an http interface should work (for example a local intranet should work). As for securely and efficiently, we are currently implementing authentication support in the SDK to allow you to bring your own authentication implementation to your agents.

author: pstephengoogle
raw_text:
I would think that this type of solution requires some custom event trigger in your agent itself. For example, you can use a cron like solution to send a new message to the same context to proceed with an update.

I don't think that this would be well represented as an input-required state, because as you said, this state is waiting for external events to continue.

In A2A I would expect this to be modeled as:
Task 0: Create the re-occuring solution to trigger the work
Task 1->N: For each day, a new task is created to perform the work of capturing the latest price information and communicating any  updates

These would all exist under one context for one user:agent pairing, but each action is its own Task with its own lifecycle

author: pstephengoogle
raw_text: Thanks for your feedback. It was discussed, at length, the value (or not) of a type-safe agent. It is not the primary use case for A2A and we have proposed a mechanism to support this, although likely cumbersome for the client to utilize. In an agentic ecosystem, some participant will have to take the role of inference to the supported schema. We believe, in general, this is better done by the agent itself that is responsible for the actions, otherwise the agent is closer to a traditional API than an agent. That being said, it is possible to allow the agent card to define some fixed schema to use in interacting. As the effort to provide protocol extensions matures, this might be a good fit without introducing this at the core protocol level.

author: kthota-g
raw_text:
1) We have an action item to determine if lastChunk can be removed. In fact, the helper for assembling the TaskArtifactUpdateEvents (https://github.com/google-a2a/a2a-python/blob/9b91fffaf723bc954617190259f46be59e9f89e5/src/a2a/utils/helpers.py#L50) doesn't use the lastChunk attribute. We will provide an update in the upcoming versions of the spec.
2) artifact.id should be the primary discriminator for an artifact. Name and description are associated metadata for the artifact. In the helper function, we only look at the parts attribute for the subsequent updates and append to the previous parts. But you can define a custom helper for a custom behavior for assembling the updates.

author: ajoslin103
raw_text:
I have handled dependencies in-line with added prompting or context as needed, but it's an interesting idea

How might we implement dependency management for such an amorphous, loosely coupled, population?

Difficult things are not by definition bad

One of my first thoughts after seeing LLMs was - I'll never write complex branching logic again.  We'll define everything as atomic units of work and the LLMs will decide what to do in which order.  In one project I changed prompting and the LLM started calling the agent multiple times and assembling results

Ok, so when I'm building systems I have dep-management for packages - at compile time -- but what would runtime dep-management look like...   

I can easily think of cases were dep-checks might avoid errors and improve results by decreasing thrashing

It'd work best in closed systems where the agents are all known, and slightly better in mixed systems, but how about when all the agents are remote?

Would we also extend dep-management into Agents that front for other Agents...

How might we establish ownership and avoid issues w/forked agents given that many of them will be closed-source?

Hmmm...

author: mikumifa
raw_text:
In fact, what I truly want is a **dependency check (dep-checks)** feature. But to be clear: It's about the description of potential dependencies.

it’s simply an indication to the user that *this agent might require certain capabilities during its operation*.

 In essence, it's part of the agent’s metadata, but with a focus on what it might **need** rather than what it **does**.

Why is this important? Here’s a concrete example: when I want to register an agent into a service registry such as **Nacos**, it would be extremely helpful if the agent carried descriptive metadata about its potential dependencies. This way, system administrators or orchestration frameworks can **preemptively ensure those capabilities are available**, avoiding runtime errors or degraded performance.

This becomes **critical** when maintaining a large, multi-agent collaborative system.

Furthermore, regardless of whether the agent is part of a **closed system**, is accessed **remotely**, or is **open- or closed-source**, having a self-declared description of its dependencies allows the broader system to **prepare in advance**. Many agents, for instance, require some form of authentication or authorization—knowing that ahead of time matters.

This mechanism **does not weaken** the LLM’s orchestration capabilities—in fact, it **enhances** them. It allows the LLM to plan, reason, and compose agents more efficiently and reliably. Especially in an ecosystem of agents with **uncertain trust boundaries or unpredictable behaviors**, **explicit dependency declarations** are a crucial step toward enabling **self-managing, resilient systems**.

author: ajoslin103
raw_text:
“In essence, it's part of the agent’s metadata, but with a focus on what it might need rather than what it does”

I'm not sure we should be limiting it that way. Because when I think more about your idea it fits with the general concept of making things more "human" and they're really aren't any limits when it comes to talking to humans, so why should there be for our agents?

And I totally agree, we need some way to pass information outside of the LLM conversation. 

When I explain it all to people these days, the difference between AI and all of the computer code that ever came before it:  I can say, we used to live in a yes/no, true/false world. Now with LLMs, we have a yes/no/maybe world. And I don't want to pass crucial information in a statement that can be maybe-ed.

Consider the MCP protocol, when I look at that and I compare it to what I'm trying to do right now I find a huge gap in it because there is no way for the MCP server to return, out of band, requirements for approval of the task. I do not want a prompt to be able to convince the LM that it doesn't need to get Human approval to perform a task. 

I don't think that we can know all of the problems and all of the things that MCP server might wanna do before it does it. I don't understand why they didn't put in any way to say I need your approval to continue. Outside of the text (in-band) communication, which is subject to prompting.

I am trying to do both local Human in the loop (the person at the keyboard) parentheses and remote Human through some external approval service/process.  I'm really hoping that the AG-UI protocol either succeeds or a superseded by a better one.

When we hear about what models are gonna be doing, and the directions that they're going to be going in over the next year. It's all about being more "human" they're gonna have more emotional intelligence. They're gonna have more “feeling”. So I think it's fine, or even a good idea, if we speak about the out of band communication still in more humanistic terms

So we probably shouldn't limit the communication with the agents to not include communication with their back ends, the MCP servers

Because the agent could be relating the capabilities of its backend, MCP servers for now, who knows what's next?

That's enough for the moment, I wouldn't blame you to use AI to summarize that

🤣

I guess what I mean, in short, is that an agent could have a dependency on Human approval for instance?  That have to come from the supporting software. Basically, the agent will be like a diplomat – it will say I can do this. And you might use it for that. You'll certainly want to do such and so forth before doing that other thing.  

Actually, I guess that means that we will want to give the agent the chance to use an LLM to describe, in-band, what it can do for other agents. We do not want to create a dependency on an out-of-band communication.  While at the same time, implementing an actual enforcement of the metadata.

This is a fantastic thing to think about, thank you so much. I appreciate you!

author: mikeas1
raw_text:
Overall your understanding is pretty much accurate. I'll address some comments inline below:

> While items (5) and (6) on your list suggest Ext-2.0 extensions can require modifying request/response format, I think you did not mean to limit extensions to such use cases. At least, it is unclear to me what it should cover and what its limitations might be. You may have intended to leave it vague intentionally to broaden the possible ways to extend the protocol?

You are correct that the proposal I have made is not limited to just modifying request/responses. Adding new methods, as you are intending with your proposal, is also supported. The details of what exactly an extension is intended to do are left to the extension profile specification (a document that defines what the extension is, how to implement it, and how it's used).

> Items (5) and (6) in your message suggest that (some) Ext-2.0 extensions can be invoked by the A2AServer when they are listed in the extensions prop of the requests. In this sense, they are more like server middleware (executed before and after the agent handler).

The client including `extensions` in a request is not required for an extension to be activated. But otherwise your assessment is correct: extensions as middleware is a very reasonable approach. However, it's not the only way. An extension could declare a separate endpoint that's used for processing extension-related requests, which would then be totally outside the standard A2A request path.

> Ext-1.0 proposes specific ways of adding custom APIs to A2A servers, enabling A2A libraries (e.g., the official A2A SDK) to implement a common logic for adding extension (e.g., def add_extension(...)). This allows for individual extension source code to be external to both A2A libraries and A2A agents. So, extensions can be easily shared and used as third-party dependencies.

I don't see anything in your proposal specific to the SDK side of this, but my thought is that the same logic applies: adding an extension should be as simple as calling `add_extension` on an A2A server. Extension developers can publish libraries that implement the logic for their extension, which should include simple ways of incorporating the extension logic (be it middleware or separate API routes) into both A2A servers and clients. There is an aspect of SDK design to this to allow easy extension hooks, which the A2A team has been discussing.

Addressing your specific questions:

> Does the AgentExtension.params prop include general or user-specific parameters? Or both?

I'm not entirely sure what you mean by user-specific parameters (perhaps you mean changing the content of `params` based on authentication provided in the HTTP request?), but I have been expecting extension configuration to be general. It's about providing information to clients that configures the way in which they interact with the server, via both requests and responses. To take your `TaskHistory` extension as an example, you could specify a separate root endpoint used for retrieving task history, which the client must then use when making TaskHistory requests, or you could specify the maximum time that a Task is kept in storage.

That said, the "extended" card could expose user-specific parameters, as the intention is that retrieving that card requires authentication. This allows the server to produce parameters specific to the caller.

> Can params be updated post-deployment? If yes (tying to the previous question), how can the client determine which of the parameters they are allowed to change?

This is a question that is applicable to all of the AgentCard, and the protocol does not dictate how AgentCards are versioned and changed. But, in general, I'd consider all parameters mutable, just as the rest of the AgentCard is mutable. We provide a free-form `version` field on the AgentCard, and I would recommend that implementers update that string when they make breaking changes.

> And following up on the previous one, if params are mutable, this means that any service that caches AgentCards (including A2A agents and registries) would have to set different TTLs for extensionful vs. extensionless agents. Or they would need to implement smarter caching logic. Maybe this is not a problem if, in the future, the A2A spec changes .well-known/agent.json to be dynamic (and all AgentCards are considered dynamic).

The protocol doesn't take a specific stance on the cacheability of AgentCards as a whole, but I'd recommend that implementers follow standard HTTP best practices: set cache control headers. If an agent developer wants to ensure that clients never cache their AgentCard, indicate that in a header.  I don't believe extensions meaningfully change this.

author: meguminnnnnnnnn
raw_text:
I encountered another question regarding the resubscribe mechanism after reviewing the Python SDK implementation.

My understanding is that resubscribe registers a new queue. Subsequently, events generated by the agent are written to both the original queue(s) and this new queue. Crucially, events that were already generated and written before the resubscribe request are not replayed into the new queue. Is this understanding correct?

Based on this, I have two follow-up questions:

- Event Timing & Potential Missed Events on Reconnect: The interval between agent events is primarily determined by LLM token generation time or local code execution time, which is typically much shorter than network latency. Therefore, if an SSE connection breaks and the client attempts to reconnect, there's a high probability that a significant number of events could be generated and written to the queues during the disconnection period. Since the resubscribed client only receives events from the new queue, all events generated during the outage window are permanently missed.
Critically, the volume of missed events could be substantial, especially under unstable networks where detection/resubscribe delays are prolonged.

- Deployment Scalability Limitation: While the QueueManager could potentially be implemented using a database, the EventQueue instances themselves appear to be local/in-memory objects managed by the agent/server process. If this is the case, doesn't this fundamentally limit the a2a server to single-machine deployments? Since the event queues are local, multiple server instances couldn't share the state of active queues or guarantee delivery to clients connected to different instances.

author: pstephengoogle
raw_text:
For your follow up questions:

- The first event on a new subscription should be the current task status, followed by subsequent update events. This means that any stream can reconstruct the Task and its state moving forward. It is unclear if there is any value to recreating the entire state evolution, if this does become necessary introduction of task status update history might be warranted
- The EventQueue is an in-process construct, yes. But for a scalable solution, this would be populated by pulling events from an event generation job. For example job A produces events as part of the agent execution. These events are pushed onto the EventQueue owned by job A but also be written to a distributed event system (e.g. pubsub or kafka). A client makes a subscription request for updates for this task, which is handled by job B. Job B then consumes the events from the distributed event stream, which are pushed onto the in-process EventQueue on job B. The QueueManager abstraction is intended to be adapted to support this type of distrubuted producer/consumer patterns, without the logic of the different event producers and consumers having to have different implementations (like AgentExecutors or subscription/push notification handlers). Of course, the proof will be when such an implementation is made, but this was considered in how the system was designed.

author: pstephengoogle
pstephengoogle
raw_text:
Thanks for raising this. We understand that the transition from 0.1 to 0.2 was a breaking one and agree that the following needs to be done to move into a smoother path moving forward:
1) Introduce protocol versions into the client and agent solutions to allow them to gracefully work across protocol versions (where possible) [#652]
2) Improve communication about breaking changes moving forward. We are using pypi versioning with the SDK and the samples should be tied to specific sdk versions. We will also use branches for protocol level breaking changes and announce them to the community with time for feedback and adaptation. For the 0.1 -> 0.2 we did use a `next` branch but didn't adequately communicate that to the community. That was our mistake and we apologize.
3) Ensure that samples are based on official SDKs and documentation is updated appropriately.

We don't expect to be make as large of changes as were done from 0.1 -> 0.2. These were based on several learnings and feedback from various implementors, as well as the work to deliver a comprehensive SDK. Our focus is to make this stable ASAP so that A2A can be a trusted part of the agent ecosystem moving forward.

author: pstephengoogle
raw_text:
There are a few things at play here:
- The expectation is that the agent you are interacting with is responsible for the conversation history, and is associated with the context_id that it returns with any response (Message or Task). Most of the frameworks are built with this assumption and it makes sense to assume this. What a remote agent returns to the client may not encompass the full history of information as known by the agent.
- Messages contain parts. You could provide the context as a DataPart in the message itself. Similarly you can provide it in the metadata, like you've done here. I think either way, is, however, not in the spirit of the interoperability goals. When you approach it this way, you have tightly coupled the client and the server expectations of structure. Passing the same contextual metadata may be ignored by other agents, or may be omitted by other clients. 

With all this, we do understand that practical implementations needs to be able to support these types of customized behaviors. To enable this in a more principled manner, we are introducing the idea of extensions [#716]. With an extension, you would be able to declare that your agent wants the history provided, and the client can send it in a manner consistent with the agents expectations. It would be an excellent test of the extensions proposal to see if you can utilize that approach to address your needs.

author: pstephengoogle
raw_text: Yes, these types of interactions is exactly what the A2A protocol is designed to support. By having agent A return a 'input-required' state with the information it is looking for, the host agent can determine (via its logic and implementation) where to turn to gather that information. In your example, it would decide agent B is the appropriate source. Similarly it could decide that the human user is the appropriate source. Both as valid and supported behaviors

author: pstephengoogle
raw_text:
This is a common ask and we are working on the right approaches generally. In particular there are worries around the card/registry aspect. Proposal is upcoming for a registry service.

As for serving multiple agents from one service. There are multiple ways to achieve this. https://github.com/google-a2a/a2a-python/pull/109 is a proposed PR to add this to the SDK

author: pstephengoogle
raw_text:
Thanks for this proposal. I think there is definite value in supporting the management of multiple tasks.

A proposal to support custom extensions has been made [#716] Your use case might be an excellent example of implementing a custom extension, in particular the idea of attaching the agents 'identity' to the response so a different component can send the follow up interactions.

As for 1) and 2) this might be better managed by your own tracking system which ties the messageId you provide in your original request to the tracking constructs you have. In the case of Tasks, the original message is part of the history and the id provided by the client is in the task message history so there is the ability to trace the interactions across jobs.

As I write this, I realized, however, that in the case of a response Message, instead of a Task, there is no indicator of the incoming request message, as it is assumed this is a direct blocking interaction, where the caller knowns the request and couples it to the response. The jsonrpc framework does provide a request and response id that are aligned, but the core A2A data model does not.

author: yasonk
raw_text:
> Thanks for this proposal. I think there is definite value in supporting the management of multiple tasks.
> 
> As for 1) and 2) this might be better managed by your own tracking system which ties the messageId you provide in your original request to the tracking constructs you have. In the case of Tasks, the original message is part of the history and the id provided by the client is in the task message history so there is the ability to trace the interactions across jobs.
> 

Thank for the analysis. When I'm saying broadcast, I'm actually thinking of ESB pattern. To support ESB, where agents are completely decoupled from one another, A2A could just have a wrapper around the Response types where it forces you to include a RequestID.

Having the ESB manage tracking of requests/response doesn't work that well. If ESB sends two Requests to the same Agent, when responding, the AGENT would need to specify that it is only responding to the second Request. Therefore I wouldn't be able to plug in a generic A2A agent into the BUS and have it function seemlessly.

author: rajeshvelicheti
raw_text:
There are multiple patterns for identifying the right agent. Some of the sample use cases are listed below

1. The host agent in the sample you referred to, uses the instructions to the llm to determine the agent. Agents are registered on the demo ui.
2. [a2a_mcp sample](https://github.com/google-a2a/a2a-samples/tree/main/samples/python/agents/a2a_mcp) uses embeddings of the agent card to match with a task. For example, the agent card that could help with hotel reservations could be matched with, book a hotel in New York for 3 nights starting 12th May.
3. A registry (WIP)

Hope this helps, there are other patterns, like an agent catalog backed by a graph connected at the skills, a database backed agent store etc.

author: kirmorozov
raw_text:
Let’s discuss a real-world use case:

I am developing a customer support system for an e-commerce company that sells products online. In this system, customers interact with a team of specialized agents via chat. The key requirements are:

1. **Customer Access**: Customers must only have access to the orders they have placed.
2. **Agent Responsibilities**: A composition of agents will perform actions on behalf of the customer, such as:
   - Looking up orders.
   - Checking tracking information.
   - Canceling orders.
   - Processing product return requests.
   - Handling product complaints.

3. **Agent Roles**: I envision the following specialized agents:
   - **Routing Agent**: Routes customer requests to the appropriate specialized agent.
   - **Product Expert**: Provides detailed product information.
   - **Order Expert**: Manages order-related tasks.
   - **Returns Expert**: Handles product return requests.
   - **Custom Product Expert**: Assists with customized product inquiries.

4. **System Integration**: The e-commerce system can generate tokens, enabling the creation of an MCP (Memory-Centric Architecture) to access customer data securely.

5. **Current Progress**: I have partially implemented this system using LangChain and raw tools without MCP. Currently, tools use memory IDs to retrieve customer context and fetch recent orders directly from the system. The Routing Agent categorizes requests and routes them to the relevant specialized agent for further assistance.

6. **Challenges**: I am currently struggling with two main issues:
   - **Token Propagation**: There is no clear method to pass authority or bearer tokens through MCP to modify the list of available tools or ensure MCP returns customer-specific data. So some agents must use provided tokens.
   - **A2A Transparency**: I believe A2A (Agent-to-Agent) communication needs to be transparent so that downstream MCPs can maintain the same access level granted to the managing or routing agent by the *USER*.

This system aims to provide a seamless and secure customer support experience while efficiently managing specialized tasks through a team of agents.

I have few hours over there weekend to get things moving.

P.S. Formatted by R1

author: HeetVekariya
raw_text:
@jkfnc 
### This issue is partially similar to #96 , so this is the temporary solution I find.

Okay, Let me summarize everything.

**Problem** : 
- Demo UI is lagging so much, that users get experience of nothing happening in the background.
- Navigating between different pages takes way longer time.

**Cause** : 
- Polling is happening with very low duration of the time difference, i.e. 1 sec.

---

- UI works perfectly on `MacOS` as multiple Mac users confirmed that.
- **I tried `demo/ui` on two different systems, `Windows i5` and `Windows i7`** and found this temp fix for windows.

**Solution** : 
- Run the agent(s) before starting UI to avoid the connection error.
- Start UI.
- When the UI loads, i.e. you hit `http://localhost:12000`, first of all click to **`Disable`** button on the top right corner to disable the auto refresh and reduce the polling overhead. **Wait until the polling stops, you can it in the terminal.** (**NOTE** - If you navigate between pages using url, polling time will reset to default value which is 1 second, so navigate using the UI only.)
- Add agent(s) using agent tab. 
- Jump to `Conversation` page and start new conversation.
- UI automatically refreshes whenever user sends a query, so when user asks something, UI gets updated and it gets reflected in the UI.
- To see further steps, you need to manually refresh the page using the refresh button present at the top right corner. You will something like below, until the response is available and at the end response when it is available.

author: yangyang-yyy
raw_text:
When one child agent is a streaming-output chatbot，i need host agent return all the parts. What kind of UpdateEvent does the child return? Can you give an example with stream response for this case? 

> Yes as Darrel states. The interaction with `message/send` and `message/stream` should always return EITHER a `Message` OR a `Task`. In the case of `message/send` those are the only options. For `message/stream` IF the first response is a `Task` AND the task is not in a terminal or interrupted state (completed, cancelled, failed or input-required) the stream should provide TaskStatusUpdateEvents and/or TaskArtifactUpdateEvents until a terminal/interrupted state (simplified to be detected by a final boolean on the update event).
> 
> You can think of this as these endpoints produce either a -`Message`; for one-off interactions that don't require complex lifecycle management, like answering a question about capability or clarifying the scope of work to accept
> 
> * `Task`; that contains the interactions and state of the Task at the time it is returned.
> 
> Streaming just provides the ability to observe, in real-time, all the deltas to the Task state as work is done.

author: holtskinner
raw_text:
See answer from https://github.com/google-a2a/A2A/discussions/120#discussioncomment-13199992

> Based on my understanding of the A2A protocol specification, the protocol serves as an interface to an agent, allowing other agents send prompts to an Agent, similar to a chatbot or other UIs which allows human prompt the agents. Skills in this context act more like hints to client agents about the types of services the server they can ask the agent to provide. I don’t believe explicitly linking tasks to skills is necessary or directly addresses the concerns you’ve raised, as tasks are technically just some unstructured prompts submitted to the agent.
> 
> If the server agent is properly guardrailed, it should evaluate incoming task prompts and respond appropriately, for example, rejecting tasks it cannot or should not perform with a message like, “Sorry, I cannot perform this task”. Your concern about bad actors exhausting agent resources is valid, but mapping tasks to a predefined catalog of skills doesn't seem to mitigate this risk. A bad actor could still submit resource intensive prompts or even unrelated prompts under a supported skill, bypassing any such mapping.
> 
> A robust protection must be implemented by the guardrails themselves, which should be designed to evaluate task prompts for malicious intent, or attempts to break the system.

author: swapydapy
raw_text:
This question seems more related to an SDK. The A2A protocol defines RPCs for sending message in a synchronous or streaming manner.

The ["send/message"](https://google-a2a.github.io/A2A/latest/specification/#71-messagesend) RPC accepts `message` & `metadata`. You should send required contextual data in [Message](https://google-a2a.github.io/A2A/latest/specification/#64-message-object) as parts. Maybe [DataPart](https://google-a2a.github.io/A2A/latest/specification/#653-datapart-object) best suits your needs. If there's some custom logic that the agent allows for, then you can leverage the `metadata` to send params for that custom logic.

Please go through the docs to dig deeper into protocol constructs: https://google-a2a.github.io/A2A/latest/topics/key-concepts/

author: swapydapy
raw_text:
As @darkhaniop pointed out, scheduled tasks is an additional capability which should be implemented as an extension to A2A. Not all agent devs need to be burdened with supporting this as requirement for A2A. The main takeaway for A2A is to give standard protocol for agents to collaborate with each other and fulfill a goal.

As mentioned previously, scheduling can be implemented by client and they can better manage what level of complexity they need.

author: swapydapy
raw_text: Also that would be better isolation of concerns. The value-add of a scheduler is to carry out an action in future. This doesn;t have any overlap with agents as such, hence better to keep the A2A protocol as nimble as possible.

author: swapydapy
raw_text:
+1 @ashwin63 

A tool is a very restrictive case of an agent. It is:

1. Deterministic in terms of what it can do. Like an API which does one thing.
2. Has pre-defined structured inputs & outputs.
3. Always succeeds or fails. There's no back and forth.

Tools are important lower level layer which is much more deterministic, hence allows for more control.

A2A allows for **collaboration** to happen between Agents to execute broader level goals:

1. **Capability negotiation**: Clients can ask the agent whether it can accomplish what they need. Agent card do define some pre-defined capabilities, but they are hints.
2. **No pre-defined input & output schema**: Agent can ask for inputs based on the task.
3. **Handle more data needed**: Agents can ask for more inputs, if needed.
4. **Recover from errors**: Agents can propose solutions and ask for user input to proceed further.

So for the case 2: The agent in orchestration layer is tasked to achieve a goal. The end result of orchestration node would be to successfully complete the goal. It can:

1. Call one or more predefined tool(s) to do it. Those tools could be scripts, APIs, predefined static workflows.
2. Or offload it to an agent using A2A and collaborate with it to finish it.

The static workflows internally can be agentic, where they use agents to complete a node in a worflow (like generate banner for text). But overall its still a tool, as it is deterministic in input/output schema and always succeeds or fails.

author: swapydapy
raw_text:
The samples are just for demo purposes of A2A constructs. The security guidelines mentioned in the specs are just added to kick off the considerations when deploying a production grade agent.

Simple validationToken is one basic strategy for push URL checks. Identity of the client should be further verified using secure mechanisms like cryptographic keys, certs etc.

General security principles around API and data exfiltration would apply. Developers are responsible for hardening their agents and securely handling inputs/outputs.

author: laurentsimon
raw_text: I just want to point out that demo code is copied and serves as samples for most developers.

author: swapydapy
raw_text:
The client for A2A server can be both: another agent or a user app.

A server creates a Task to allow the client to track its execution. `TaskUpdateEvent` indicates that status of the task has changed. It could be completed, failed. input-required etc. The [TaskStatus](https://google-a2a.github.io/A2A/latest/specification/#62-taskstatus-object) contains both the `state` & optional `Message`. The agent can populate this message to indicate some context around the current status.

When a task is finished, agent populates the results of the task as [Artifacts](https://google-a2a.github.io/A2A/latest/specification/#67-artifact-object).

A task can go from `submitted`, `working` states to `completed`. Its up to you, if you want to show the intermediate state changes to the user.

While the agent is generating the artifacts, it can send `TaskArtifactUpdateEvent`s stream.

1. You can use the TaskArtifactUpdateEvent, to maintain a copy of the artifacts on the client side and show it on the user. This gives realtime experience.
2. Or wait for the task to reach `completed` state, and use getTask API to get full Task object and all the artifacts.

author: swapydapy
raw_text:
Agree with @MengJiapeng . In the current spec, a complete part object would be sent in one `TaskArtifactUpdateEvent`.

Since there's no partId to uniquely identify a part, there's no way to identify chunks, belonging to same-part, across `TaskArtifactUpdateEvent` events.

author: swapydapy
raw_text:
The agent developer uses A2A API protocol to expose agentic, collaborative experience. How they implement it internally is upto them and opaque to the clients. If they use an LLLM, whether they allow client to tweak the system instructions (think security implications) and how, is not guaranteed for all agent devs. Hence this is not part of A2A protocol.

If the agent devs still wish to support it, possibly in a more restricted and business contextual way, this could be implemented as an extension to current A2A protocol. Please checkout: https://google-a2a.github.io/A2A/latest/topics/extensions/

> When a task is finished, agent populates the results of the task as [Artifacts](https://google-a2a.github.io/A2A/latest/specification/#67-artifact-object).
> 
> A task can go from `submitted`, `working` states to `completed`. Its up to you, if you want to show the intermediate state changes to the user.
> 
> While the agent is generating the artifacts, it can send `TaskArtifactUpdateEvent`s stream.
> 
> 1. You can use the TaskArtifactUpdateEvent, to maintain a copy of the artifacts on the client side and show it on the user. This gives realtime experience.
> 2. Or wait for the task to reach `completed` state, and use getTask API to get full Task object and all the artifacts.

Got it. Thanks.

author: lukehinds
raw_text:
Assume you're using python:

There are two methods:

- `"message/send"`: which uses the `SendMessageRequest` model
- `"message/stream"`: which uses `SendStreamingMessageRequest` 

Message itself is neither stream or non stream specific , its just a model to hold 


```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/ \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "jsonrpc": "2.0",
    "method": "message/send",
    "params": {
      "message": {
        "role": "user",
        "parts": [{"text": "Hello from A2A client"}],
        "messageId": "msg-001",
        "contextId": "ctx-001"
      }
    },
    "id": "req-001"
  }'
```

### Streaming Message
```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/ \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "jsonrpc": "2.0",
    "method": "message/stream",
    "params": {
      "message": {
        "role": "user", 
        "parts": [{"text": "Hello from A2A client - Stream a response"}],
        "messageId": "stream-001",
        "contextId": "stream-ctx"
      }
    },
    "id": "stream-req"
  }'
```

Message is just a container for a single message exchanged between user and agent.

Hope that helps, apologies if I misunderstood your question.

author: jsahil
raw_text:
Is A2A Really Meant for Serverless Architecture?

We have a host agent running on one Lambda, and remote agents running on separate Lambdas in our current serverless architecture. However, we are facing limitations due to cold starts — both on the host Lambda and the remote agents — which leads to significant response delays and, at times, even timeouts.

Given these challenges, we’re looking for guidance on how to achieve seamless performance with A2A in a serverless setup. Is this architecture feasible for agentic workflows, or would a server-based architecture be the more reliable option for managing these workflows?

Any insights or recommendations would be greatly appreciated!

author: mikeas1
raw_text:
Thanks for this report. I think you stated the problem pretty clearly: when agents provide some authorization token in-band, the receiving agent should have some way to prove that it came from the user they expected. We may also need confidentiality.

As of now, our advice has been to always provide these authorizations out-of-band, exactly for the reasons you've detailed. OAuth works well here, since the agent that requires authorization can generate an authorization URL that ultimately sends the authorization code to the agent itself. That's kind of the encrypted tunnel you've discussed, just using HTTPS and standard OAuth flows, it's just not specified how that takes place. We leave the decision up to the implementers for other ways of representing authorization -- if you need to collect an API key from a user, you could have your agent expose a web page with a form to collect the key and return a link to that page for the user to navigate to.

Most of these decisions (auth in-band or out-of-band, how an agent decides that it requires additional authorization, how you bind bearer tokens to specific bearers) are up to the agent developer, and don't specifically impact A2A. We can provide guidance on best practices, but I would not encode specific authorization flows into the protocol as requirements.

Let me outline the solutions you proposed and try to connect those to the protocol:

1. Message signatures. We could have some way in the protocol for passing signatures on messages. Part of this is potentially identifying how and where public keys come from (ephemeral, persistent, tied to a user identity, tied to an agent identity, etc). If we had this, the Agent implementation could verify that in-band authorization came from the source it expected it to.
2. A protocol for establishing a direct channel between two parties. We could have some way of transferring information required for setting up a private channel between agents.

My feeling is that option 2 is too specific to include in the core protocol overall. An extension could be used for defining the details of how that information is exchanged. For Agents that need that level of security, they could implement such an extension.

Option 1 is potentially something that the base protocol could adopt. It's possible right now with extensions (you could place a signature in the `metadata` field), but if it becomes a very common need, adding a `signature` field to a `Message` or `Artifact` seems reasonable.

I'm not sure about message encryption. It seems like the idea there is that you could have a chain of agents and you want to tunnel a message from one end of that chain to another. The problem is that an intermediate agent receiving the encrypted message would need to figure out what to do with it (i.e. where to route the message). I suppose if you had both confidential and non-confidential parts, intermediate agents could make routing decisions on the non-confidential parts. It sounds interesting, but also seems like this would be a difficult pattern to implement correctly for every agent.

author: c-mysec
raw_text:
> Can the openai.yaml and openrpc.json be auto-generated from the existing a2a.json ?

Not a complete or accurate specification. There are some "SHOULD" clauses in the specification and the agent card doesn't have the information if the agent endpoint supports it, uses it or not. For instance:
- SHOULD use standard HTTP status codes like [401 Unauthorized](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Status/401) or [403 Forbidden](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Status/403) for authentication challenges or rejections.

The agent card has no information what status code is used and the openapi spec has the field to specify the behavior.
I wonder if a tool that auto-generate a partial/incomplete interface spec would be misleading or used in a wrong way.

author: laurentsimon
raw_text:
> I'm not sure about message encryption. It seems like the idea there is that you could have a chain of agents and you want to tunnel a message from one end of that chain to another. The problem is that an intermediate agent receiving the encrypted message would need to figure out what to do with it 

Encrypted message does not mean the recipient is unknown. It just means the intermediary cannot see the payload, thus it can still route the message. If option 1 is possible, I think it can be augmented with encryption, which "simply" requires the sender to know the public key of the recipient.

Let me know if I mis-understood something.

author: mikeas1
raw_text:
> Encrypted message does not mean the recipient is unknown

Yeah, that's fair. I suppose I'm concerned about the implications this has for agent developers, but the more I think about it, the more I realize this requirement is likely already present. I think encrypted messages just add an additional layer of complexity to it, since now you have to be careful to include decision-making content in the unencrypted portion.

In any case, I think message signing is a very reasonable addition. We might explore this via an extension to start. If you'd like to help us design that, I'd definitely welcome the help. Something like JWS would be nice due to the ubiquity, but it's not clear how we'd make that work -- a DataPart containing the JWS fields would work, but I'd prefer if signatures could be less intrusive. We could define a canonicalization for serialized `Message`s, then add a `signatures` field in the `metadata` that contains the JWS `header` and `signature`  fields. Essentially the [Verifiable Credentials Data Integrity](https://www.w3.org/TR/vc-data-integrity/) model of embedding proofs.

author: c-mysec
raw_text:
with a help from gpt4o

# Checklist: Information Needed in openapi.yaml Beyond AgentCard

This document outlines all required and recommended fields in an OpenAPI 3.1 specification (`openapi.yaml`) for an A2A-compliant agent, that **cannot be inferred directly from the AgentCard**.

---

## ✅ Checklist of Information **NOT in AgentCard** but **Required/Recommended** in `openapi.yaml`

### 1. 🔵 `info` Section (API Metadata)

| Field               | Required? | Inferred from AgentCard? | Notes |
|---------------------|-----------|---------------------------|-------|
| `title`             | ✅ Yes    | ❌ No                     | The name of the **API**, not the agent. E.g., "A2A Agent Discovery API" |
| `version`           | ✅ Yes    | ❌ No                     | The **API spec version**, not the agent version |
| `description`       | ✅ Yes    | ❌ No                     | Explains the **purpose of the API**, not the agent’s function |
| `termsOfService`    | ⚠️ Optional | ❌ No                  | Include if available |
| `contact`, `license`| ⚠️ Recommended | ❌ No              | Recommended for public/corporate APIs for governance/compliance |

### 2. 🔵 `servers` Section (Deployment Info)

| Field       | Required? | Inferred from AgentCard? | Notes |
|-------------|-----------|---------------------------|-------|
| `url`       | ✅ Yes    | ❌ No                     | Must specify where the agent is hosted |
| `variables` | ⚠️ Optional | ❌ No                  | Helps support multi-environment configs (e.g., staging/prod) |

### 3. 🔵 `paths` Section (REST Endpoints)

| Field                   | Required? | Inferred from AgentCard? | Notes |
|-------------------------|-----------|---------------------------|-------|
| `/well-known/agent.json`| ✅ Yes    | ❌ No                     | But it is specified in the standard |
| `operationId`           | ⚠️ Recommended | ❌ No                | Useful for codegen and interactive docs |
| `tags`                  | ⚠️ Recommended | ❌ No                | Helps group endpoints in tools like Swagger UI |

### 4. 🔵 `responses` (Beyond 200)

| Status | Inferred from AgentCard? | Notes |
|--------|---------------------------|-------|
| 401    | ❌ No                    | Missing or invalid credentials |
| 403    | ❌ No                    | Authenticated but lacks permission |
| 500    | ❌ No                    | Unexpected internal errors |

### 5. 🔵 `security` (at the Operation Level)

| Field     | Inferred from AgentCard? | Notes |
|-----------|---------------------------|-------|
| `security`| ⚠️ Partially              | `AgentCard` lists available schemes, but not which ones are required per endpoint — this must be explicitly stated in the spec |

### 6. 🔵 Error Schemas and Examples

| Field                 | Inferred? | Notes |
|----------------------|-----------|-------|
| `components.schemas.ErrorResponse` | ❌ No | Recommended for public APIs |
| `examples`            | ⚠️ Partially | If `skills[].examples` are present, they can be reused — but more full usage examples may be needed for context |

### 7. 🔵 Vendor Extensions (If Applicable)

| Extension                   | Needed? | Inferred? | Notes |
|----------------------------|---------|-----------|-------|
| `x-logo`, `x-agent-id`, `x-runtime` | ⚠️ Optional | ❌ No | Useful for visual tools like Stoplight or Redoc |
| `x-a2a-supported-version`  | ⚠️ Recommended | ❌ No | Indicates compatibility with A2A protocol version — useful in multi-agent environments |

---

## ✅ Summary — What You **Must Provide** Beyond the `AgentCard`

1. **API metadata**: `info.title`, `info.version`, `info.description`, `contact`
2. **Agent base URL**: e.g., `https://your-agent.com`
3. **Required security schemes per endpoint** (e.g., Bearer only, or multiple)
4. **Expected HTTP status codes** beyond 200: `401`, `403`, `500`

author: inesmcm26
raw_text:
> Can we instead rename to partId? that way the purpose of a unique identifier is much more clearer. "Name" provides a human-readable string, and doesn't necessarily imply uniqueness. 

Thanks for the suggestion @swapydapy! I get the concern about `name` not implying uniqueness, and I agree that `partId` would better communicate that.

My goal with `name` is to keep things intuitive and clear for developers working on agents and agent executors. Since agents are typically invoked using fixed parameters names like `query` or `context`, having human-readable labels for each part makes mapping them to the agent’s invocation arguments straightforward and unambiguous.
Something like a UUID-style `partId` would be harder to work with in practice.

Just to clarify: were you thinking of `partId` as something auto-generated and unique like a UUID, or more of a developer-defined label?
