author: kthota-g
raw_text:
Agents can determine whether to respond back to the user query as Message objects or Task objects. 

In general, Message type is used for communicating simple, stateless events back to the user in both streaming and non-streaming cases. For example, the [helloworld example](https://github.com/a2aproject/a2a-samples/blob/main/samples/python/agents/helloworld/agent_executor.py) returns a Message object which is sent over SSE as below.

```
data: {"id":33,"jsonrpc":"2.0","result":{"kind":"message","messageId":"6e679c66-a5a4-4536-9d74-95f36fcdd95a","parts":[{"kind":"text","text":"Hello World"}],"role":"agent"}}
```

TaskArtifactUpdateEvent allows for serving the artifacts in chunks and has the appropriate attributes such as append, lastChunk, artifactId required to identify the artifacts associated.

author: kthota-g
raw_text:
Few thoughts on this:

I see Agent Catalog as a component of Registry and not part of a A2A Protocol or A2AServer itself. There will be scenarios where Agent cards are exposed via different runtimes (e.g. agents running in different ecosystems, different servers etc.) that span more than just one A2AServer that need to be added to a catalog to facilitate their discovery.

[This page](https://a2aproject.github.io/A2A/latest/topics/agent-discovery/) discusses various discovery mechanisms. If an A2AServer is exposing an agent, it can use well-known url to publish its agent card. Alternatively, an A2AServer can expose agent cards at custom urls and leverage direct configuration/private discovery for those agents.

For scenarios where multiple agent cards need to be exposed for discovery, a curated registry service would act as the catalog server and the registry server would address how the resources in a catalog would be exposed to the clients. The specification to adopt to support this functionality is to be determined, api-catalog or any other spec that suits well considering additional requirements such as policy enforcement (e.g. entitlements to determine what agents are available for a client).

author: ognis1205
raw_text:
Hey @kthota-g , thanks for sharing your thoughts — I think you raise several important points, especially around heterogeneous agent deployments and the potential role of a (centralized) registry service.

That said, I’d like to offer a small clarification regarding the intent behind using the API Catalog (RFC 9727) **format** for the Agent Catalog.

The schema is specifically designed to support **Same-Origin, Multi-Agent** configurations through **open discovery**, where a single server (or gateway) hosts multiple agents under subpaths (e.g., `/a2a/agent1`, `/a2a/agent2`) and exposes a catalog at a well-known URL `/.well-known/api-catalog`.

In this context, the term **"Agent Catalog"** follows the naming convention established by **API Catalog** in RFC 9727 — it refers to **a standardized format and discovery surface**, **not a service-level registry or general-purpose cataloging service**. This distinction is important: while centralized registries or entitlement-aware catalogs have their own use cases, the Agent Catalog is scoped specifically for open discovery on shared origins.

That said, I do share some of your intuition — especially when considering whether the Agent Catalog should be viewed as part of the A2A protocol itself, or as a more loosely coupled discovery mechanism layered on top.

Happy to continue the discussion around how we might layer those additional capabilities while keeping compatibility with the standard where possible.

author: austin-tral
raw_text:
This feature would be amazing! I want to add my support.

A2A servers are stateful by design because they expose task history via the `tasks/get` method. However, without a way of listing task IDs, A2A clients are forced to be stateful as well. The clients must store task IDs themselves or have some other mechanism for task recall.

The `tasks/list` method will help unlock stateless clients:
1. No need for the client to persist any task metadata between disconnects/reconnects. Any client can continue a conversation by listing tasks (`tasks/list`), getting task history (`tasks/get`), and then sending a message (`message/send`).
2. Multiple clients can contribute to a singular task without sharing any task metadata/state. (e.g. you can start a conversation with an agent from a browser app and continue it in a CLI).

Fully support this. It's high-priority for my team.

author: inesmcm26
raw_text:
That's a fair question. Wrapping everything in a `DataPart` (even other nested parts) is possible, but I think it undermines the strength of the multi-part message protocol.

The real issue is clarity. A `TextPart` says “this is text,” a `FilePart` says “this is a file,” and a `DataPart` says “this is some arbitrary structured thing, go figure it out”. If you move everything into a `DataPart`, suddenly you need extra logic on the agent executor side just to figure out what’s what: is this a string? A nested part? A file reference? You lose clarity, and add parsing overhead.

With multi-part messages, you’re guaranteed a list of known typed parts. You can iterate over them, process them consistently, and the agent executor doesn’t have to guess or parse nested structures. Adding a `name` field to each part lets us preserve that clarity while also allowing agent executors to map message parts to agent inputs.

Does that make sense? What do you think about it?

author: swapydapy
raw_text:
I think we are talking about two things:

1. Mapping message parts to agent inputs or outputs: This is something specific to an agent, and agent would advertise that it needs a specific message structure for input like that to work. At a general protocol level, I feel DataPart is more suited to send pre-defined & structured inputs to the agent. And since agent has already advertised that it needs what inputs it needs, client should sent that in message DataPart. There's no guessing required on the agent side, since agent already know which keys mean what. Same is the case for artifact, defined keys should be sent in DataPart.

2. Clear type of general message contents: This is exactly what part types are for. Allows the consumer (client or agent) of a message to know what is the type of the message part.

At a general level (without any pre-defined structure), for a multi-part message, consumer would need to infer the intent for each part.

So the question is, what is the purpose of part-name, possibly:
1. Semantic hint on the contents of the part. Helps the consumer to infer the intent of the part.
2. Human readable name.

Eg: https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A/discussions/796#discussioncomment-13606595

Artifact: Flight booking. Agent didn't pre advertise how it generates artifacts for a flight book. If they had, I would suggest, that stuff goes into DataPart.

Part A: (TextPart, name="summary") Summary of the flight, seats type etc.
Part B: (TextPart, name="directions") Directions on how to reach airport, how to hotel once your reach.
Part C: (FilePart, name="invoice") PDF invoice of the flight booking.
Part D: (FilePart, name="travel restrictions & baggage") baggage allowance, and restricted items
Part D: (DartPart) Ticket PNR, Time of flights, flight No, Seat No.
